# The Great Mosque of Damascus / Umayyad Mosque (706-715 CE) - Children's Content

## 🏛 Fun Fact

The Umayyad Mosque in Damascus is one of the most beautiful mosques in the world! Caliph al-Walid ibn Abd al-Malik built it for almost 10 years (706-715 CE). What's interesting is that this mosque was built on the site of what used to be Saint John Church belonging to Christians.

Caliph al-Walid did not take the church by force, but conducted good negotiations. He offered to build several new churches elsewhere and provide substantial compensation. Finally, Christians agreed and surrendered their church. This shows high tolerance in Islam!

🎨 *Illustration: 8th-century Damascus with Caliph al-Walid sitting in consultation with Christian leaders in a palace garden. There are city maps and building sketches on the table. A calm and respectful dialogue atmosphere. Setting: palace garden with Islamic and Christian architecture, green garden colors, golden palace, blue sky. Peaceful and diplomatic atmosphere.*

---

## 🔧 Construction Process

The construction of the Umayyad Mosque required the best architects and artists from various countries. There were experts from Byzantium (present-day Turkey), Persia (present-day Iran), and Arabs working together to create a unique design. They combined different architectural styles into one beautiful unity.

The finest materials were brought from throughout the Mediterranean region. Marble and granite columns were selected one by one to ensure strength and beauty. The most special feature was the mosaics - millions of pieces of colored glass and gold arranged to form pictures of heavenly gardens with beautiful rivers.

The architectural team also created important innovations such as a ventilation system that made cool air circulate well, and an acoustic system that made the imam's voice clearly heard throughout the mosque. The *mihrab* design not only showed the *qiblah* direction, but also amplified sound.

🎨 *Illustration: Architects from various nations (Arab, Persian, Byzantine) working together with ancient architectural tools. Some are measuring, drawing, and installing colorful mosaics. Building materials are scattered around the construction site. Setting: construction site with wooden scaffolding, marble stones, and colorful mosaic pieces. Atmosphere of international cooperation and high expertise.*

---

## 🌍 Beauty and Impact

The mosaics of the Umayyad Mosque are considered one of the finest works of Islamic art throughout history. Using millions of pieces of colored glass and gold, these mosaics depict heavenly scenes with gardens, rivers, and beautiful palaces. There are no human or animal images, in accordance with Islamic art principles.

Arabic calligraphy adorning the mosque walls displays Quranic verses about the beauty of Allah's creation. This mosque became not only a place of worship, but also a center for education, social activities, and politics. Scholars taught here, merchants traded around the mosque, and the community gathered for various events.

The Umayyad Mosque became a model for building grand mosques throughout the Islamic world. The architectural style developed here influenced mosque designs in Andalusia, North Africa, and Central Asia. The concept of combining beauty with practical function became a standard in mosque architecture.

🎨 *Illustration: The magnificent interior of the Umayyad Mosque with colorful mosaics on the walls, tall marble columns, and congregants from various tribes praying. Golden light enters through windows, illuminating beautiful Arabic calligraphy. Setting: a vast and tall mosque space with mixed architecture, dominant colors of golden mosaics, white marble, green carpet. Devout and magnificent atmosphere.*

---

## 📖 Meaning and Lessons

The Umayyad Mosque teaches many valuable lessons. First, the importance of dialogue and tolerance between religions. Caliph al-Walid showed that religious differences do not have to result in conflict, but can be resolved through fair and respectful negotiation.

Second, Islam values beauty and art. The beautiful mosaics and amazing calligraphy show that Islam encourages its followers to create noble works of art. Allah SWT says:

> وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ فَادْعُوهُ بِهَا ﴿١٨٠﴾
>
> *"And to Allah belong the best names, so invoke Him by them."* ﴾180﴿

Third, the importance of international cooperation. Architects from various nations worked together to create an extraordinarily beautiful mosque. This shows that cultural diversity can enrich the treasury of Islamic arts and architecture.

🎨 *Illustration: Night view of Damascus with the Umayyad Mosque illuminated by soft light. There are Muslim and Christian children playing together in the city park, while adults from various religions trade peacefully in the night market. Setting: multicultural city with Islamic and Christian architecture side by side, warm colors of night lights and stars. Harmonious atmosphere of tolerance.*
masjid-agung-damaskus-masjid-umayyah.children-id:
# Masjid Agung Damaskus / Masjid Umayyah (706-715 M) - Konten Anak-Anak

## 🏛 Fun Fact

Masjid Umayyah di Damaskus adalah salah satu masjid terindah di dunia! Khalifah al-Walid ibn Abdul Malik membangunnya selama hampir 10 tahun (706-715 M). Yang menarik, masjid ini dibangun di tempat yang dulunya adalah Gereja Saint John milik umat Kristen.

Khalifah al-Walid tidak mengambil gereja secara paksa, tetapi melakukan negosiasi yang baik. Beliau menawarkan untuk membangun beberapa gereja baru di tempat lain dan memberikan kompensasi yang besar. Akhirnya, umat Kristen setuju dan menyerahkan gereja mereka. Ini menunjukkan toleransi yang tinggi dalam Islam!

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Damaskus abad ke-8 dengan Khalifah al-Walid duduk bermusyawarah dengan pemimpin Kristen di sebuah taman istana. Ada peta kota dan sketsa bangunan di atas meja. Suasana dialog yang tenang dan saling menghormati. Setting: taman istana dengan arsitektur Islam dan Kristen, warna hijau taman, emas istana, biru langit. Suasana damai dan diplomatic.*

---

## 🔧 Proses Pembangunan

Pembangunan Masjid Umayyah membutuhkan arsitek dan seniman terbaik dari berbagai negara. Ada ahli dari Bizantium (Turki sekarang), Persia (Iran sekarang), dan Arab yang bekerja sama menciptakan desain yang unik. Mereka memadukan gaya arsitektur yang berbeda-beda menjadi satu kesatuan yang indah.

Material terbaik didatangkan dari seluruh wilayah Mediterania. Kolom-kolom dari marmer dan granit dipilih satu per satu untuk memastikan kekuatan dan keindahan. Yang paling istimewa adalah mosaik - jutaan potongan kaca berwarna dan emas yang disusun membentuk gambar taman-taman surga dengan sungai-sungai yang indah.

Tim arsitek juga menciptakan inovasi penting seperti sistem ventilasi yang membuat udara sejuk bersirkulasi dengan baik, dan sistem akustik yang membuat suara imam terdengar jelas di seluruh masjid. Desain mihrab tidak hanya menunjukkan arah kiblat, tetapi juga memperkuat suara.

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Para arsitek dari berbagai bangsa (Arab, Persia, Bizantium) bekerja bersama dengan alat-alat arsitektur kuno. Ada yang mengukur, menggambar, dan memasang mosaik warna-warni. Material bangunan berserakan di sekitar lokasi konstruksi. Setting: lokasi pembangunan dengan scaffolding kayu, batu marmer, dan potongan mosaik berwarna-warni. Suasana kerjasama internasional dan keahlian tinggi.*

---

## 🌍 Keindahan dan Dampak

Mosaik Masjid Umayyah dianggap sebagai salah satu karya seni Islam terbaik sepanjang masa. Menggunakan jutaan potongan kaca berwarna dan emas, mosaik ini menggambarkan pemandangan surga dengan taman-taman, sungai-sungai, dan istana-istana yang indah. Tidak ada gambar manusia atau hewan, sesuai dengan prinsip seni Islam.

Kaligrafi Arab yang menghiasi dinding-dinding masjid menampilkan ayat-ayat Al-Quran tentang keindahan ciptaan Allah. Masjid ini tidak hanya menjadi tempat ibadah, tetapi juga pusat pendidikan, sosial, dan politik. Para ulama mengajar di sini, pedagang berdagang di sekitar masjid, dan masyarakat berkumpul untuk berbagai acara.

Masjid Umayyah menjadi model untuk pembangunan masjid-masjid besar di seluruh dunia Islam. Gaya arsitektural yang dikembangkan di sini mempengaruhi desain masjid di Andalusia, Afrika Utara, dan Asia Tengah. Konsep menggabungkan keindahan dengan fungsi praktis menjadi standar dalam arsitektur masjid.

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Interior Masjid Umayyah yang megah dengan mosaik berwarna-warni di dinding, kolom marmer tinggi, dan jamaah dari berbagai suku sedang shalat. Cahaya emas masuk melalui jendela-jendela, menerangi kaligrafi Arab yang indah. Setting: ruang masjid yang luas dan tinggi dengan arsitektur campuran, warna dominan emas mosaik, putih marmer, hijau karpet. Suasana khusyuk dan megah.*

---

## 📖 Makna dan Pelajaran

Masjid Umayyah mengajarkan banyak pelajaran berharga. Pertama, pentingnya dialog dan toleransi antar agama. Khalifah al-Walid menunjukkan bahwa perbedaan agama tidak harus menghasilkan konflik, tetapi bisa diselesaikan dengan negosiasi yang adil dan saling menghormati.

Kedua, Islam menghargai keindahan dan seni. Mosaik yang indah dan kaligrafi yang mengagumkan menunjukkan bahwa Islam mendorong umatnya untuk menciptakan karya seni yang mulia. Allah SWT berfirman:

> وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ فَادْعُوهُ بِهَا ﴿١٨٠﴾
>
> *"Dan hanya milik Allah nama-nama yang baik (Asmaul Husna), maka bermohonlah kepada-Nya dengan menyebut nama-nama itu."* ﴾180﴿

Ketiga, pentingnya kerjasama internasional. Para arsitek dari berbagai bangsa bekerja sama menciptakan masjid yang luar biasa indah. Ini menunjukkan bahwa keragaman budaya dapat memperkaya khazanah seni dan arsitektur Islam.

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Pemandangan malam Damaskus dengan Masjid Umayyah yang diterangi cahaya lembut. Ada anak-anak Muslim dan Kristen bermain bersama di taman kota, sementara orang dewasa dari berbagai agama berdagang dengan damai di pasar malam. Setting: kota multikultural dengan arsitektur Islam dan Kristen berdampingan, warna hangat cahaya malam dan bintang. Suasana harmonis dan toleransi.*
masjid-agung-damaskus-masjid-umayyah.general-en:
# The Great Mosque of Damascus / Umayyad Mosque (706-715 CE)

## Political and Cultural Context of Damascus

Damascus as the capital of the Umayyad Caliphate required an architectural symbol that reflected the magnificence and legitimacy of the ruling dynasty.^1 **Caliph al-Walid ibn Abd al-Malik** (705-715 CE) understood that building a grand mosque was not only a spiritual need, but also a political statement about the power and progress of Islamic civilization.^1

The city of Damascus itself had a long history as a center of civilization.^2 When Muslim forces conquered this city in 636 CE under the command of **Khalid ibn al-Walid**,^1 they found a mature city structure with various monumental buildings, including the Church of Saint John which would become an important part in the history of the Umayyad Mosque.^2

## Negotiations with the Christian Community

One of the most interesting aspects in the construction of the Umayyad Mosque was the negotiation process between Caliph al-Walid and the Christian community of Damascus.^3 Since the conquest, Christians and Muslims shared the Saint John Church complex for worship.^3 This church had magnificent Byzantine architecture and a strategic position in the heart of the city.^3

Al-Walid proposed to purchase the entire church complex to be converted into a mosque.^4 Initially, the Christian community refused because this church had high historical and spiritual value for them.^4 However, the caliph offered very attractive compensation: construction of several new churches in other locations within the city, plus substantial financial compensation.^4

After lengthy negotiations, an agreement was reached in 706 CE.^5 The Christian community agreed to surrender the church complex under the agreed conditions.^5 This showed a high level of tolerance and diplomacy in interfaith relations during the Umayyad period.^5

## Design Process and Architectural Innovation

Al-Walid invited the best architects and artists from various regions of the caliphate and even from outside Islamic territories.^6 The architectural team consisted of Byzantine, Persian, and Arab experts who created a unique architectural style synthesis.^6 They combined traditional elements with new innovations suitable for Islamic worship needs.^6

One of the most important innovations was the *mihrab* design that not only functioned as a *qiblah* direction indicator, but also as an acoustic element that amplified the imam's voice.^7 The ventilation and lighting systems were designed in such a way that cool air could circulate well in the vast space, while natural light entered through specially designed windows.^7

The use of mosaics as the main decorative element was an adaptation of Byzantine tradition, but with motifs appropriate to Islamic aesthetic principles.^8 There were no human or animal images, but geometric motifs, calligraphy, and abstract natural landscapes were used.^8

## Construction Phases

The construction of the Umayyad Mosque lasted almost a decade (706-715 CE) and was divided into several strategic phases.^5 The first phase (706-708 CE) included partial demolition of the old church structure and foundation strengthening.^5 Architects had to be careful to preserve structural elements that could still be used.^5

The second phase (708-712 CE) was the construction of the main mosque structure, including the main prayer hall with dimensions of 136 x 37 meters.^5 ^6 Installation of marble and granite columns brought from various Mediterranean regions.^5 ^6 Each column was carefully selected to ensure structural strength and visual harmony.^6

The final phase (712-715 CE) was the completion of ornaments and decorations.^7 This was the most spectacular phase, where thousands of square meters of mosaics were installed by expert artists.^7 Arabic calligraphy with Quranic verses was placed in strategic positions, creating a deep spiritual atmosphere.^7

## The Marvel of Mosaics and Ornamentation

The mosaics of the Umayyad Mosque are considered one of the finest achievements of Islamic art throughout history.^7 Using millions of pieces of colored glass and gold, these mosaics depict heavenly scenes with rivers, gardens, and beautiful palaces.^7 The manufacturing technique adapted the skills of Byzantine artists but with Islamic aesthetic vision.^7

The calligraphy adorning the mosque walls displays selected verses from the Quran, especially those related to Allah's magnificence and the beauty of His creation.^8 One of the most prominent verses is:

> وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ فَادْعُوهُ بِهَا ﴿١٨٠﴾
>
> *"And to Allah belong the best names, so invoke Him by them."* ﴾180﴿
> — QS. Al-A'raf (7): 180

## Social and Cultural Impact

The completion of the Umayyad Mosque in 715 CE created widespread impact in Damascus society and throughout the caliphate territories.^2 This mosque functioned not only as a place of worship, but also as a center for social, educational, and political activities.^2 Scholars used the mosque spaces for teaching, while merchants conducted business transactions in the area around the mosque.^2

Culturally, the Umayyad Mosque became a model for the construction of grand mosques throughout the Islamic world.^6 The architectural style developed here influenced mosque designs in Andalusia, North Africa, and Central Asia.^6 The concept of integration between aesthetic beauty and practical function became a standard in mosque architecture.^6

## Legacy and Continuity

Despite experiencing various damages and renovations throughout history, including earthquakes and fires, the Umayyad Mosque has maintained its basic structure and architectural character.^2 Each renovation period sought to preserve the integrity of the original design while making necessary technical improvements.^2

The scholarly tradition also developed around this mosque.^2 The library built in the mosque complex became one of the most important learning centers in the Middle Ages.^2 Scholars from various disciplines, from *fiqh* to mathematics, gathered and exchanged ideas here.^2

## Wisdom and Reflection

The construction of the Umayyad Mosque shows how Islam can integrate cultural diversity while maintaining strong spiritual identity. The negotiations with the Christian community exemplified high diplomacy and religious tolerance.

**Important values that can be learned:**
- **Interfaith dialogue** can produce win-win solutions
- **Architectural excellence** reflects the level of civilization of a society
- **Cultural integration** enriches the treasury of Islamic arts and architecture
- **Long-term investment** in spiritual infrastructure provides sustainable benefits

---

## Bibliography

1. Al-Baladhuri, Ahmad ibn Yahya, *Futuh al-Buldan*, Beirut: Maktabah al-Hilal, 1988.
2. Ibn Asakir, Ali ibn al-Hasan, *Tarikh Madinat Dimashq*, Beirut: Dar al-Fikr, 1995.
3. Al-Ya'qubi, Ahmad ibn Abi Ya'qub, *Tarikh al-Ya'qubi*, Beirut: Dar al-Kutub al-Ilmiyyah, 1999.
4. Ibn Kathir, Ismail ibn Umar, *Al-Bidayah wa al-Nihayah*, Riyadh: Dar Tayyibah, 1997.
5. Creswell, K.A.C., *Early Muslim Architecture*, Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1969.
6. Grabar, Oleg, *The Formation of Islamic Art*, New Haven: Yale University Press, 1987.
7. Flood, Finbarr Barry, *The Great Mosque of Damascus: Studies on the Makings of an Umayyad Visual Culture*, Leiden: Brill, 2001.
8. Al-Qur'an al-Karim. QS. Al-A'raf (7): 180.
masjid-agung-damaskus-masjid-umayyah.general-id:
# Masjid Agung Damaskus / Masjid Umayyah (706-715 M)

## Konteks Politik dan Budaya Damaskus

Damaskus sebagai ibu kota Kekhalifahan Umayyah memerlukan simbol arsitektural yang mencerminkan kemegahan dan legitimasi dinasti yang berkuasa.^1 **Khalifah al-Walid ibn Abdul Malik** (705-715 M) memahami bahwa pembangunan masjid agung bukan hanya kebutuhan spiritual, tetapi juga pernyataan politik tentang kekuatan dan kemajuan peradaban Islam.^1

Kota Damaskus sendiri memiliki sejarah panjang sebagai pusat peradaban. Ketika pasukan Muslim menaklukkan kota ini pada tahun 636 M di bawah komando **Khalid ibn al-Walid**,^1 mereka menemukan struktur kota yang sudah matang dengan berbagai bangunan monumental, termasuk Gereja Saint John yang akan menjadi bagian penting dalam sejarah Masjid Umayyah.^2

## Negosiasi dengan Komunitas Kristen

Salah satu aspek paling menarik dalam pembangunan Masjid Umayyah adalah proses negosiasi antara Khalifah al-Walid dengan komunitas Kristen Damaskus.^3 Sejak masa penaklukan, umat Kristen dan Muslim berbagi kompleks Gereja Saint John untuk beribadah.^3 Gereja ini memiliki arsitektur Byzantine yang megah dan posisi strategis di jantung kota.^3

Al-Walid mengajukan proposal untuk membeli seluruh kompleks gereja untuk dijadikan masjid.^4 Awalnya, komunitas Kristen menolak karena gereja ini memiliki nilai historis dan spiritual yang tinggi bagi mereka.^4 Namun, khalifah menawarkan kompensasi yang sangat menarik: pembangunan beberapa gereja baru di lokasi lain dalam kota, plus kompensasi finansial yang besar.^4

Setelah negosiasi yang panjang, kesepakatan tercapai pada tahun 706 M.^5 Komunitas Kristen setuju untuk menyerahkan kompleks gereja dengan syarat-syarat yang telah disepakati.^5 Ini menunjukkan tingkat toleransi dan diplomasi yang tinggi dalam hubungan antar agama pada masa Umayyah.^5

## Proses Desain dan Inovasi Arsitektural

Al-Walid mengundang arsitek dan seniman terbaik dari berbagai wilayah kekhalifahan dan bahkan dari luar wilayah Islam.^6 Tim arsitektur terdiri dari ahli Byzantine, Persia, dan Arab yang menciptakan sintesis gaya arsitektural yang unik.^6 Mereka memadukan elemen-elemen tradisional dengan inovasi-inovasi baru yang sesuai dengan kebutuhan ibadah Islam.^6

Salah satu inovasi terpenting adalah desain mihrab yang tidak hanya berfungsi sebagai penunjuk arah qiblat, tetapi juga sebagai elemen akustik yang memperkuat suara imam.^7 Sistem ventilasi dan pencahayaan dirancang sedemikian rupa sehingga udara sejuk dapat bersirkulasi dengan baik di dalam ruang yang luas, sementara cahaya alami masuk melalui jendela-jendela yang dirancang khusus.^7

Penggunaan mosaik sebagai elemen dekoratif utama merupakan adaptasi dari tradisi Byzantine, tetapi dengan motif-motif yang sesuai dengan prinsip-prinsip estetika Islam.^8 Tidak ada gambar manusia atau hewan, tetapi menggunakan motif geometris, kaligrafi, dan pemandangan alam yang abstrak.^8

## Fase-Fase Konstruksi

Pembangunan Masjid Umayyah berlangsung selama hampir satu dekade (706-715 M) dan dibagi dalam beberapa fase strategis.^5 Fase pertama (706-708 M) meliputi pembongkaran sebagian struktur gereja lama dan penguatan fondasi.^5 Arsitek harus berhati-hati untuk mempertahankan elemen-elemen struktural yang masih dapat digunakan.^5

Fase kedua (708-712 M) adalah konstruksi struktur utama masjid, termasuk ruang shalat utama dengan dimensi 136 x 37 meter.^5 ^6 Pemasangan kolom-kolom dari marmer dan granit yang didatangkan dari berbagai wilayah Mediterania.^5 ^6 Setiap kolom dipilih dengan cermat untuk memastikan kekuatan struktural dan keharmonisan visual.^6

Fase terakhir (712-715 M) adalah penyelesaian ornamen dan dekorasi.^7 Inilah fase yang paling spektakuler, di mana ribuan meter persegi mosaik dipasang oleh seniman-seniman ahli.^7 Kaligrafi Arab dengan ayat-ayat al-Quran ditempatkan pada posisi-posisi strategis, menciptakan atmosfer spiritual yang mendalam.^7

## Keajaiban Mosaik dan Ornamentasi

Mosaik Masjid Umayyah dianggap sebagai salah satu pencapaian seni Islam terbaik sepanjang masa.^7 Menggunakan jutaan potongan kaca berwarna dan emas, mosaik ini menggambarkan pemandangan surga dengan sungai-sungai, taman-taman, dan istana-istana yang indah.^7 Teknik pembuatannya mengadaptasi keahlian seniman Byzantine tetapi dengan visi estetika Islam.^7

Kaligrafi yang menghiasi dinding-dinding masjid menampilkan ayat-ayat pilihan dari al-Quran, terutama yang berkaitan dengan kemegahan Allah dan keindahan ciptaan-Nya.^8 Salah satu ayat yang paling menonjol adalah:

> وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ فَادْعُوهُ بِهَا ﴿١٨٠﴾
>
> *"Dan hanya milik Allah nama-nama yang baik (Asmaul Husna), maka bermohonlah kepada-Nya dengan menyebut nama-nama itu."* ﴾180﴿
> — QS. Al-A'raf (7): 180

## Dampak Sosial dan Budaya

Penyelesaian Masjid Umayyah pada tahun 715 M menciptakan dampak yang luas dalam masyarakat Damaskus dan seluruh wilayah kekhalifahan.^2 Masjid ini tidak hanya berfungsi sebagai tempat ibadah, tetapi juga sebagai pusat kegiatan sosial, pendidikan, dan politik.^2 Para ulama menggunakan ruang-ruang masjid untuk mengajar, sementara pedagang melakukan transaksi bisnis di area sekitar masjid.^2

Secara budaya, Masjid Umayyah menjadi model bagi pembangunan masjid-masjid besar di seluruh dunia Islam.^6 Gaya arsitektural yang dikembangkan di sini mempengaruhi desain masjid-masjid di Andalusia, Afrika Utara, dan Asia Tengah.^6 Konsep integrasi antara keindahan estetika dan fungsi praktis menjadi standar dalam arsitektur masjid.^6

## Warisan dan Kontinuitas

Meski mengalami berbagai kerusakan dan renovasi sepanjang sejarah, termasuk gempa bumi dan kebakaran, Masjid Umayyah tetap mempertahankan struktur dasar dan karakter arsitekturalnya.^2 Setiap periode renovasi berusaha memelihara integritas desain asli sambil melakukan perbaikan teknis yang diperlukan.^2

Tradisi ilmiah juga berkembang di sekitar masjid ini.^2 Perpustakaan yang dibangun di kompleks masjid menjadi salah satu pusat pembelajaran terpenting pada Abad Pertengahan.^2 Para sarjana dari berbagai disiplin ilmu, mulai dari fiqh hingga matematika, berkumpul dan bertukar pikiran di sini.^2

## Hikmah dan Refleksi

Pembangunan Masjid Umayyah menunjukkan bagaimana Islam dapat mengintegrasikan keragaman budaya sambil mempertahankan identitas spiritual yang kuat. Negosiasi dengan komunitas Kristen mencontohkan diplomasi dan toleransi beragama yang tinggi.

**Nilai-nilai penting yang dapat dipetik:**
- **Dialog antarumat beragama** dapat menghasilkan solusi yang win-win
- **Keunggulan arsitektural** mencerminkan tingkat peradaban sebuah masyarakat
- **Integrasi budaya** memperkaya khazanah seni dan arsitektur Islam
- **Investasi jangka panjang** dalam infrastruktur spiritual memberikan manfaat berkelanjutan

---

## Daftar Pustaka

1. Al-Baladhuri, Ahmad ibn Yahya, *Futuh al-Buldan*, Beirut: Maktabah al-Hilal, 1988.
2. Ibn Asakir, Ali ibn al-Hasan, *Tarikh Madinat Dimashq*, Beirut: Dar al-Fikr, 1995.
3. Al-Ya'qubi, Ahmad ibn Abi Ya'qub, *Tarikh al-Ya'qubi*, Beirut: Dar al-Kutub al-Ilmiyyah, 1999.
4. Ibn Kathir, Ismail ibn Umar, *Al-Bidayah wa al-Nihayah*, Riyadh: Dar Tayyibah, 1997.
5. Creswell, K.A.C., *Early Muslim Architecture*, Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1969.
6. Grabar, Oleg, *The Formation of Islamic Art*, New Haven: Yale University Press, 1987.
7. Flood, Finbarr Barry, *The Great Mosque of Damascus: Studies on the Makings of an Umayyad Visual Culture*, Leiden: Brill, 2001.
8. Al-Qur'an al-Karim. QS. Al-A'raf (7): 180.

masjid-agung-cordoba-mezquita.children-en:
# The Great Mosque of Cordoba / Mezquita (784 CE) - Children's Content

## 🌍 What Happened?

In 756 CE, an Umayyad prince named Abd al-Rahman I successfully escaped from the massacre of his family by the Abbasid dynasty. He arrived in Andalusia (present-day Spain) and established a new kingdom. His grandson, Abd al-Rahman II, decided to build a very beautiful mosque in the city of Cordoba in 784 CE.

This mosque was built on the site of what used to be Saint Vincent Church belonging to Christians. Unlike other places, in Cordoba Christians and Muslims had long shared the same place of worship since 711 CE. When a grand mosque was to be built, good negotiations were conducted between the two communities.

🎨 *Illustration: Map of 8th-century Andalusia showing Abd al-Rahman I's journey from Damascus to his arrival in Cordoba. Arrows show the long and dangerous travel route. There are ships on the Mediterranean Sea and caravans on land. Setting: ancient map with brown parchment colors, blue sea, green land. Atmosphere of adventure and new hope.*

---

## 📖 The Construction Story

The most interesting aspect of the Cordoba Mosque was its unprecedented architectural innovation. The architects created a unique system of columns and double arches that had never existed before. Lower arches supported upper arches, so the ceiling could be high and look very magnificent.

The architects cleverly used old columns from Roman and Visigoth times found in ancient building ruins. Each column was different - some tall, some short, some with different motifs - but they all harmonized when united. This shows that diversity can create beauty.

The most famous feature was the zebra pattern on the arches - red from brick and white from limestone. This pattern was not only beautiful to look at, but also had an important structural function to support the large building.

🎨 *Illustration: Interior of Cordoba Mosque with hundreds of columns and double arches forming a forest of columns. The red-white zebra pattern is very striking. There are workers from various nations carefully installing stones. Setting: vast mosque space with tall columns, dominant colors of red brick, white limestone, brown wood. Atmosphere of hard work and high skill.*

---

## 🌟 Wonders and Development

The Cordoba Mosque was not built all at once, but developed in several phases over hundreds of years. Each ruler added new sections that became increasingly beautiful. Abd al-Rahman III added a tall minaret when he proclaimed himself caliph, showing his political power.

The most spectacular was the expansion by Al-Hakam II who added a new *mihrab* with extraordinarily beautiful mosaics. He even brought special artists from Constantinople (present-day Istanbul) to create these mosaics. Imagine, Byzantine Christian artists working for an Islamic mosque!

This mosque also had the world's largest library at that time, with thousands of books in various languages: Arabic, Latin, Greek, and Hebrew. Famous scholars like Ibn Rushd (Averroes) often taught and discussed here. The mosque was not only a place of worship, but also a center of knowledge.

🎨 *Illustration: The Cordoba Mosque library with tall shelves containing thousands of manuscripts. There are scholars from various nations sitting reading and discussing. Byzantine artists are working on beautiful mosaics in the mihrab. Setting: magnificent library room with soft light, golden manuscript colors, blue mosaics, brown wooden shelves. Calm and knowledge-filled atmosphere.*

---

## 🤲 Prayer & Wisdom

The Cordoba Mosque teaches us about resilience and strong vision. Abd al-Rahman and his family did not give up when facing difficulties, but built a new civilization that was even more beautiful than before. Allah SWT says:

> أَفَمَنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَانَهُ عَلَىٰ تَقْوَىٰ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَرِضْوَانٍ خَيْرٌ أَم مَّنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَانَهُ عَلَىٰ شَفَا جُرُفٍ هَارٍ ﴿١٠٩﴾
>
> *"Is one who laid the foundation of his building on righteousness [with fear] from Allah and [seeking] His approval better or one who laid the foundation of his building on the edge of a bank about to collapse?"* ﴾109﴿

This mosque also shows that diversity is beautiful. Different columns actually created harmony. Artists from various religions and nations worked together to create extraordinary works of art. Islam appreciates skill and beauty from wherever they come.

🎨 *Illustration: Night view of Cordoba with the Cordoba Mosque as the illuminated city center. There are people from various religions and nations walking peacefully in the city streets. Stars shine brightly in the Andalusian sky. Setting: Islamic-Andalusian city with harmonious architecture, warm colors of night lights, golden mosque lamps, blue starry night. Atmosphere of peace and tolerance.*
masjid-agung-cordoba-mezquita.children-id:
# Masjid Agung Cordoba / Mezquita (784 M) - Konten Anak-Anak

## 🌍 Apa yang Terjadi?

Pada tahun 756 M, seorang pangeran Umayyah bernama Abd al-Rahman I berhasil melarikan diri dari pembantaian keluarganya oleh dinasti Abbasiyah. Ia tiba di Andalusia (Spanyol sekarang) dan mendirikan kerajaan baru. Cucunya, Abd al-Rahman II, memutuskan untuk membangun masjid yang sangat indah di kota Cordoba pada tahun 784 M.

Masjid ini dibangun di tempat yang dulunya adalah Gereja Saint Vincent milik umat Kristen. Berbeda dengan tempat lain, di Cordoba umat Kristen dan Muslim sudah lama berbagi tempat ibadah yang sama sejak tahun 711 M. Ketika akan dibangun masjid besar, dilakukan negosiasi yang baik antara kedua komunitas.

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Peta Andalusia abad ke-8 menunjukkan perjalanan Abd al-Rahman I dari Damaskus hingga tiba di Cordoba. Panah menunjukkan rute perjalanan yang panjang dan berbahaya. Ada kapal di laut Mediterania dan karavan di daratan. Setting: peta kuno dengan warna coklat parchment, biru laut, hijau daratan. Suasana petualangan dan harapan baru.*

---

## 📖 Cerita Pembangunan

Yang paling menarik dari Masjid Cordoba adalah inovasi arsitekturnya yang belum pernah ada sebelumnya. Para arsitek menciptakan sistem kolom dan lengkungan ganda (double arches) yang sangat unik. Lengkungan bawah menopang lengkungan atas, sehingga langit-langit bisa tinggi dan terlihat sangat megah.

Para arsitek pintar-pintar menggunakan kolom-kolom bekas dari zaman Romawi dan Visigoth yang ditemukan di reruntuhan bangunan kuno. Setiap kolom berbeda-beda, ada yang tinggi pendek, ada yang motifnya berbeda, tapi semuanya harmonis ketika disatukan. Ini menunjukkan bahwa keberagaman bisa menciptakan keindahan.

Yang paling terkenal adalah pola zebra pada lengkungan - warna merah dari batu bata dan putih dari batu kapur. Pola ini bukan hanya indah dipandang, tapi juga memiliki fungsi struktural yang penting untuk menopang bangunan yang besar.

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Interior Masjid Cordoba dengan ratusan kolom dan lengkungan ganda yang membentuk hutan kolom. Pola zebra merah-putih sangat mencolok. Ada pekerja dari berbagai bangsa sedang memasang batu dengan hati-hati. Setting: ruang masjid yang luas dengan kolom-kolom tinggi, warna dominan merah batu bata, putih batu kapur, coklat kayu. Suasana kerja keras dan keahlian tinggi.*

---

## 🌟 Keajaiban dan Perkembangan

Masjid Cordoba tidak dibangun sekali jadi, tetapi berkembang dalam beberapa fase selama ratusan tahun. Setiap penguasa menambahkan bagian baru yang semakin indah. Abd al-Rahman III menambahkan menara yang tinggi ketika ia memproklamirkan diri sebagai khalifah, menunjukkan kekuatan politiknya.

Yang paling spektakuler adalah ekspansi oleh Al-Hakam II yang menambahkan mihrab baru dengan mosaik yang luar biasa indah. Ia bahkan mendatangkan seniman khusus dari Konstantinopel (Istanbul sekarang) untuk membuat mosaik ini. Sungguh menakjubkan, seniman Kristen Bizantium bekerja untuk masjid Islam!

Masjid ini juga memiliki perpustakaan terbesar di dunia pada zamannya, dengan ribuan buku dalam berbagai bahasa: Arab, Latin, Yunani, dan Ibrani. Para ulama terkenal seperti Ibn Rushd (Averroes) sering mengajar dan berdiskusi di sini. Masjid bukan hanya tempat ibadah, tapi juga pusat ilmu pengetahuan.

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Perpustakaan Masjid Cordoba dengan rak-rak tinggi berisi ribuan manuskrip. Ada ulama dari berbagai bangsa duduk membaca dan berdiskusi. Seniman Bizantium sedang mengerjakan mosaik yang indah di mihrab. Setting: ruang perpustakaan yang megah dengan cahaya lembut, warna emas manuskrip, biru mosaik, coklat kayu rak. Suasana tenang dan penuh ilmu.*

---

## 🤲 Doa & Hikmah

Masjid Cordoba mengajarkan kita tentang ketahanan dan visi yang kuat. Abd al-Rahman dan keluarganya tidak menyerah ketika menghadapi kesulitan, tetapi membangun peradaban baru yang bahkan lebih indah dari sebelumnya. Allah SWT berfirman:

> أَفَمَنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَانَهُ عَلَىٰ تَقْوَىٰ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَرِضْوَانٍ خَيْرٌ أَم مَّنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَانَهُ عَلَىٰ شَفَا جُرُفٍ هَارٍ ﴿١٠٩﴾
>
> *"Maka apakah orang yang mendirikan bangunannya atas dasar takwa kepada Allah dan keredhaan-Nya itu lebih baik, ataukah orang yang mendirikan bangunannya di tepi jurang yang runtuh?"* ﴾109﴿

Masjid ini juga menunjukkan bahwa keberagaman itu indah. Kolom-kolom yang berbeda-beda justru menciptakan keharmonisan. Seniman dari berbagai agama dan bangsa bekerja sama menciptakan karya seni yang luar biasa. Islam menghargai keahlian dan keindahan dari mana pun asalnya.

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Pemandangan Cordoba di malam hari dengan Masjid Cordoba sebagai pusat kota yang bercahaya. Ada orang-orang dari berbagai agama dan bangsa berjalan damai di jalan-jalan kota. Bintang-bintang bersinar terang di langit Andalusia. Setting: kota Islam-Andalusia dengan arsitektur yang harmonis, warna hangat cahaya malam, emas lampu masjid, biru malam berbintang. Suasana damai dan toleransi.*
masjid-agung-cordoba-mezquita.general-en:
# The Great Mosque of Cordoba / Mezquita (784 CE)

## Political Context of Andalusia

In 756 CE, **Abd al-Rahman I** (Abd al-Rahman al-Dakhil) successfully established the Emirate of Cordoba after fleeing from the massacre of the Umayyad dynasty by the Abbasids.^1 As the only survivor from the Umayyad family who managed to reach Andalusia, he had a great mission: to rebuild the magnificence of Umayyad civilization in a new land.^1

Cordoba at that time was still a relatively small provincial city compared to Damascus or Baghdad.^2 However, Abd al-Rahman I had a vision to make it a center of Islamic civilization that could rival the great capitals in the East.^2 Building a grand mosque became the top priority in realizing this vision.^2

## Background of Construction

The decision to build a grand mosque in Cordoba was not only based on the spiritual needs of Muslims, but also as a strong political statement. **Abd al-Rahman II** (822-852 CE), grandson of Abd al-Rahman I, continued the project planned by his predecessor and began construction in 784 CE.^3

The chosen location was the former Visigoth Church of Saint Vincent which had been shared between Christians and Muslims since the conquest of Cordoba in 711 CE. Unlike the Damascus case, the acquisition process in Cordoba involved more complex negotiations because both communities had long shared the same space.^4

## Unique Architectural Innovation

The Cordoba Mosque displayed architectural innovations never before seen in Islamic tradition. The system of columns and double arches became the most famous characteristic feature. This structure consisted of lower arches supporting upper arches, creating a stunning visual effect while solving the technical problem of ceiling height.^5

The architects used columns from various historical periods, including Roman and Visigoth columns found in ancient building ruins. This strategy was not only economical, but also created attractive visual diversity. Each column had unique characteristics, yet remained harmonious in the overall composition.^6

The use of red brick and white stone in the arches created the iconic zebra pattern. This pattern was not only decorative, but also had a structural function in distributing the building's load. The combination of red and white colors later became the visual signature of the Cordoba Mosque known worldwide.^7

## Expansion Phases

The Cordoba Mosque underwent several expansion phases that reflected the development of political and economic power of the Emirate, later the Caliphate of Cordoba. The initial construction during Abd al-Rahman II's time (784-786 CE) covered an area of about 4,875 square meters with 11 prayer rows (*saff*).^8

The first expansion was carried out by **Abd al-Rahman III** (929-961 CE) who extended the mosque southward and added a magnificent minaret. This expansion coincided with his proclamation of himself as caliph, affirming Cordoba's political independence from the Abbasids in Baghdad.^1

The most spectacular expansion occurred during **Al-Hakam II's** reign (961-976 CE), who added a new *mihrab* with extraordinarily beautiful mosaic decoration. Byzantine artists were specially brought from Constantinople to work on these mosaics, showing the complex diplomatic relations between the Islamic and Christian worlds during that period.^2

## Technical and Aesthetic Excellence

The lighting system of the Cordoba Mosque was designed very carefully. Small windows were strategically placed to create dramatic interplay of light and shadow. At certain times of day, sunlight created moving geometric patterns on the mosque floor, adding a spiritual dimension to the worship experience.^3

The mosque's acoustics also received special attention. The main *mihrab* was designed with deep recesses and *muqarnas* (stalactite) decoration that functioned as natural resonators. This system allowed the imam's voice to be heard clearly to the back rows, even though the mosque had a very vast space.^4

The ornamentation in the mosque displayed the evolution of Andalusian Islamic art. The firm Kufic calligraphy of the early period developed into the more fluid Naskh style in later expansions. Geometric and floral motifs (*taurid*) reached extraordinary levels of complexity, reflecting the advancement of Andalusian Islamic technique and aesthetics.^5

## Religious and Cultural Significance

The Cordoba Mosque was not only a place of worship, but also the center of Andalusia's intellectual life. The library built in the mosque complex became one of the largest in the world at that time, storing thousands of manuscripts in various languages: Arabic, Latin, Greek, and Hebrew.^6

Leading scholars and philosophers such as **Ibn Rushd (Averroes)** and **Ibn Hazm** often taught and discussed in the mosque area. The *majlis* (discussion forum) tradition that developed here influenced the development of Islamic thought, not only in Andalusia but throughout the Islamic world.^7

The mosque also became a place for various religious and state rituals. The Caliph of Cordoba used the mosque for Friday sermons that also functioned as a forum for political communication with the people. Celebrations of major Islamic holidays were held grandly in the mosque complex, strengthening the religious and cultural identity of Andalusian society.^8

## Intellectual Legacy

The scholarly tradition that developed around the Cordoba Mosque made a great contribution to world civilization. Schools established in the mosque complex produced scholars who translated and developed Greek philosophical works, Indian and Persian scientific works, and created new innovations in various disciplines.^1

The educational system developed at the Cordoba Mosque became a model for other Islamic educational institutions. A curriculum that integrated religious and secular sciences, traditions of dialogue and academic debate, and appreciation for diversity of thought became characteristic of Andalusian Islamic education.^2

## Wisdom and Reflection

The construction and development of the Cordoba Mosque shows how architectural works can become manifestations of civilizational resilience and vision. From the political ruins of the Umayyad dynasty in the East, Abd al-Rahman and his successors managed to build a new center of civilization that even surpassed the magnificence of previous capitals.

The Quran reminds us about the importance of building on strong foundations:

> أَفَمَنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَانَهُ عَلَىٰ تَقْوَىٰ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَرِضْوَانٍ خَيْرٌ أَم مَّنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَانَهُ عَلَىٰ شَفَا جُرُفٍ هَارٍ ﴿١٠٩﴾
>
> *"Is one who laid the foundation of his building on righteousness [with fear] from Allah and [seeking] His approval better or one who laid the foundation of his building on the edge of a bank about to collapse?"* ﴾109﴿
> — QS. At-Taubah (9): 109

**Values that can be learned:**
- **Resilience in facing challenges** can produce extraordinary achievements
- **Architectural innovation** reflects civilizational progress and adaptability
- **Integration of various cultural traditions** enriches artistic and intellectual treasury
- **Investment in education and knowledge** provides long-term impact for civilization

---

## Bibliography

1. Ibn al-Qutiyyah, Muhammad, *Tarikh Iftitah al-Andalus*, Madrid: Real Academia de la Historia, 1926.
2. Al-Maqqari, Ahmad ibn Muhammad, *Nafh al-Tib min Ghusn al-Andalus al-Ratib*, Beirut: Dar al-Kutub al-Ilmiyyah, 1997.
3. Ibn Hayyan, Hayyan ibn Khalaf, *Al-Muqtabis fi Tarikh Rijal al-Andalus*, Beirut: Dar al-Kutub al-Ilmiyyah, 1999.
4. Al-Himyari, Muhammad, *Al-Rawd al-Mi'tar fi Khabar al-Aqtar*, Beirut: Maktabah Lubnan, 1984.
5. Ewert, Christian, *Spanisch-islamische Systeme sich kreuzender Bögen*, Berlin: Walter de Gruyter, 1968.
6. Dodds, Jerrilynn D., *Al-Andalus: The Art of Islamic Spain*, New York: Metropolitan Museum of Art, 1992.
7. Ruggles, D. Fairchild, *Gardens, Landscape, and Vision in the Palaces of Islamic Spain*, Pennsylvania: Penn State Press, 2000.
8. Al-Qur'an al-Karim. QS. At-Taubah (9): 109.
masjid-agung-cordoba-mezquita.general-id:
# Masjid Agung Cordoba / Mezquita (784 M)

## Konteks Politik Andalusia

Pada tahun 756 M, **Abd al-Rahman I** (Abd al-Rahman al-Dakhil) berhasil mendirikan Emirat Cordoba setelah melarikan diri dari pembantaian dinasti Umayyah oleh Abbasiyah.^1 Sebagai satu-satunya penyintas dari keluarga Umayyah yang berhasil mencapai Andalusia, ia memiliki misi besar: membangun kembali kemegahan peradaban Umayyah di tanah baru.^1

Cordoba pada masa itu masih berupa kota provinsi yang relatif kecil dibandingkan dengan Damaskus atau Baghdad.^2 Namun, Abd al-Rahman I memiliki visi untuk menjadikannya sebagai pusat peradaban Islam yang dapat menyaingi ibu kota-ibu kota besar di Timur.^2 Pembangunan masjid agung menjadi prioritas utama dalam mewujudkan visi tersebut.^2

## Latar Belakang Pembangunan

Keputusan untuk membangun masjid agung di Cordoba bukan hanya didasarkan pada kebutuhan spiritual umat Islam, tetapi juga sebagai pernyataan politik yang kuat.^3 **Abd al-Rahman II** (822-852 M), cucu Abd al-Rahman I, melanjutkan proyek yang telah direncanakan pendahulunya dan memulai konstruksi pada tahun 784 M.^3

Lokasi yang dipilih adalah bekas Gereja Visigoth Saint Vincent yang telah dibagi penggunaannya antara umat Kristen dan Muslim sejak penaklukan Cordoba pada tahun 711 M.^4 Berbeda dengan kasus Damaskus, proses akuisisi di Cordoba melibatkan negosiasi yang lebih kompleks karena kedua komunitas telah lama berbagi ruang yang sama.^4

## Inovasi Arsitektural Unik

Masjid Cordoba menampilkan inovasi arsitektural yang belum pernah ada sebelumnya dalam tradisi Islam.^5 Sistem kolom dan lengkungan ganda (*double arches*) menjadi ciri khas yang paling terkenal.^5 Struktur ini terdiri dari lengkungan bawah yang menopang lengkungan atas, menciptakan efek visual yang menakjubkan sekaligus memecahkan masalah teknis ketinggian langit-langit.^5

Para arsitek menggunakan kolom-kolom dari berbagai periode sejarah, termasuk kolom Romawi dan Visigoth yang ditemukan di reruntuhan bangunan kuno.^6 Strategi ini tidak hanya ekonomis, tetapi juga menciptakan keragaman visual yang menarik.^6 Setiap kolom memiliki karakteristik unik, namun tetap harmonis dalam keseluruhan komposisi.^6

Penggunaan bata merah dan batu putih dalam lengkungan menciptakan pola zebra yang ikonik.^7 Pola ini bukan hanya dekoratif, tetapi juga memiliki fungsi struktural dalam mendistribusikan beban bangunan.^7 Kombinasi warna merah dan putih kemudian menjadi signature visual Masjid Cordoba yang dikenal di seluruh dunia.^7

## Tahapan Ekspansi

Masjid Cordoba mengalami beberapa fase ekspansi yang mencerminkan perkembangan kekuatan politik dan ekonomi Emirat, kemudian Kekhalifahan Cordoba.^3 Pembangunan awal pada masa Abd al-Rahman II (784-786 M) mencakup area seluas sekitar 4.875 meter persegi dengan 11 saff (barisan) shalat.^3

Ekspansi pertama dilakukan oleh **Abd al-Rahman III** (929-961 M) yang memperluas masjid ke arah selatan dan menambahkan menara (*minaret*) yang megah.^3 Ekspansi ini bertepatan dengan proklamasi dirinya sebagai khalifah, menegaskan independensi politik Cordoba dari Abbasiyah di Baghdad.^3

Ekspansi paling spektakuler terjadi pada masa **Al-Hakam II** (961-976 M), yang menambahkan mihrab baru dengan dekorasi mosaik yang luar biasa indah.^3 Tim seniman Bizantium didatangkan khusus dari Konstantinopel untuk mengerjakan mosaik ini, menunjukkan hubungan diplomatik yang kompleks antara dunia Islam dan Kristen pada periode tersebut.^6

## Keunggulan Teknis dan Estetis

Sistem pencahayaan Masjid Cordoba dirancang dengan sangat cermat. Jendela-jendela kecil ditempatkan secara strategis untuk menciptakan permainan cahaya dan bayangan yang dramatis. Pada waktu-waktu tertentu dalam sehari, cahaya matahari menciptakan pola-pola geometris yang bergerak di lantai masjid, menambah dimensi spiritual dalam pengalaman beribadah.^3

Akustik masjid juga mendapat perhatian khusus. Mihrab utama dirancang dengan ceruk yang dalam dan dekorasi muqarnas (*stalactite*) yang berfungsi sebagai resonator alami. Sistem ini memungkinkan suara imam terdengar jelas hingga ke barisan-barisan belakang, meski masjid memiliki ruang yang sangat luas.^4

Ornamentasi dalam masjid menampilkan evolusi seni Islam Andalusia. Kaligrafi Kufic yang tegas pada periode awal berkembang menjadi gaya Naskh yang lebih luwes pada ekspansi-ekspansi selanjutnya. Motif geometris dan floral (*taurid*) mencapai tingkat kerumitan yang luar biasa, mencerminkan kemajuan teknik dan estetika Islam Andalusia.^5

## Signifikansi Religius dan Kultural

Masjid Cordoba bukan hanya tempat ibadah, tetapi juga pusat kehidupan intelektual Andalusia. Perpustakaan yang dibangun di kompleks masjid menjadi salah satu yang terbesar di dunia pada masa itu, menyimpan ribuan manuskrip dalam berbagai bahasa: Arab, Latin, Yunani, dan Ibrani.^6

Para ulama dan filosof terkemuka seperti **Ibn Rushd (Averroes)** dan **Ibn Hazm** sering mengajar dan berdiskusi di area masjid. Tradisi *majlis* (forum diskusi) yang berkembang di sini mempengaruhi perkembangan pemikiran Islam, tidak hanya di Andalusia tetapi juga di seluruh dunia Islam.^7

Masjid juga menjadi tempat berbagai ritual keagamaan dan negara. Khalifah Cordoba menggunakan masjid untuk khutbah Jumat yang juga berfungsi sebagai forum komunikasi politik dengan rakyat. Perayaan hari-hari besar Islam dilakukan dengan meriah di kompleks masjid, memperkuat identitas keagamaan dan budaya masyarakat Andalusia.^8

## Warisan Intelektual

Tradisi keilmuan yang berkembang di sekitar Masjid Cordoba memberikan kontribusi besar bagi peradaban dunia. Sekolah-sekolah yang didirikan di kompleks masjid menghasilkan para sarjana yang menerjemahkan dan mengembangkan karya-karya filosofi Yunani, karya-karya sains India dan Persia, serta menciptakan inovasi-inovasi baru dalam berbagai disiplin ilmu.^1

Sistem pendidikan yang dikembangkan di Masjid Cordoba menjadi model bagi institusi-institusi pendidikan Islam lainnya. Kurikulum yang mengintegrasikan ilmu-ilmu agama dan umum, tradisi dialog dan debat akademis, serta penghargaan terhadap keragaman pemikiran menjadi ciri khas pendidikan Islam Andalusia.^2

## Hikmah dan Refleksi

Pembangunan dan perkembangan Masjid Cordoba menunjukkan bagaimana karya arsitektural dapat menjadi manifestasi dari resiliensi dan visi peradaban. Dari reruntuhan politik dinasti Umayyah di Timur, Abd al-Rahman dan para penerusnya mampu membangun pusat peradaban baru yang bahkan melampaui kemegahan ibu kota-ibu kota sebelumnya.

Al-Quran mengingatkan kita tentang pentingnya membangun dengan landasan yang kuat:

> أَفَمَنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَانَهُ عَلَىٰ تَقْوَىٰ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَرِضْوَانٍ خَيْرٌ أَم مَّنْ أَسَّسَ بُنْيَانَهُ عَلَىٰ شَفَا جُرُفٍ هَارٍ ﴿١٠٩﴾
>
> *"Maka apakah orang yang mendirikan bangunannya atas dasar takwa kepada Allah dan keredhaan-Nya itu lebih baik, ataukah orang yang mendirikan bangunannya di tepi jurang yang runtuh?"* ﴾109﴿
> — QS. At-Taubah (9): 109

**Nilai-nilai yang dapat dipetik:**
- **Resiliensi dalam menghadapi tantangan** dapat menghasilkan pencapaian yang luar biasa
- **Inovasi arsitektural** mencerminkan kemajuan peradaban dan kemampuan adaptasi
- **Integrasi berbagai tradisi budaya** memperkaya khazanah seni dan intelektual
- **Investasi dalam pendidikan dan ilmu pengetahuan** memberikan dampak jangka panjang bagi peradaban

---

## Daftar Pustaka

1. Ibn al-Qutiyyah, Muhammad, *Tarikh Iftitah al-Andalus*, Madrid: Real Academia de la Historia, 1926.
2. Al-Maqqari, Ahmad ibn Muhammad, *Nafh al-Tib min Ghusn al-Andalus al-Ratib*, Beirut: Dar al-Kutub al-Ilmiyyah, 1997.
3. Ibn Hayyan, Hayyan ibn Khalaf, *Al-Muqtabis fi Tarikh Rijal al-Andalus*, Beirut: Dar al-Kutub al-Ilmiyyah, 1999.
4. Al-Himyari, Muhammad, *Al-Rawd al-Mi'tar fi Khabar al-Aqtar*, Beirut: Maktabah Lubnan, 1984.
5. Ewert, Christian, *Spanisch-islamische Systeme sich kreuzender Bögen*, Berlin: Walter de Gruyter, 1968.
6. Dodds, Jerrilynn D., *Al-Andalus: The Art of Islamic Spain*, New York: Metropolitan Museum of Art, 1992.
7. Ruggles, D. Fairchild, *Gardens, Landscape, and Vision in the Palaces of Islamic Spain*, Pennsylvania: Penn State Press, 2000.
8. Al-Qur'an al-Karim. QS. At-Taubah (9): 109.

masjid-agung-samarra-menara-malwiya.children-en:
# The Great Mosque of Samarra and Malwiya Tower: Marvel of Abbasid Architecture

## 🏛 Fun fact

The Malwiya Tower in Samarra was the world's first mosque tower with a spiral shape! It stands 52 meters tall with a winding path that allows people to climb to the top without using stairs. Even donkeys could climb to the top because of its gentle and wide slope.

The Great Mosque of Samarra was once the largest mosque in the world at its time, covering 38,000 square meters. That's about the size of 5 football fields! This mosque could accommodate tens of thousands of worshippers at once for congregational prayers.

Samarra city itself was built from scratch by Caliph Al-Mu'tasim in 836 CE as the new capital of the Abbasid Caliphate. The name "Samarra" comes from Arabic "Surra man ra'a" which means "happy is he who sees it."

🎨 *Illustration: Aerial panorama of Samarra city showing the mosque complex with the spiral Malwiya Tower as the center of attention. The city stretches along the Tigris River with palaces, gardens, and magnificent buildings. Setting: 9th-century Iraq with clear blue sky. Dominant colors: brown brick, green gardens, blue river, golden domes. Mood: magnificent and amazing. Composition: aerial view wide shot with Malwiya Tower as focal point.*

---

## 🔬 Explanation

Construction of the Great Mosque of Samarra began in 848 CE and was completed in 852 CE under the command of Caliph Al-Mutawakkil. The architects used very advanced construction techniques, combining fired brick with high-quality lime mortar.

The mosque walls were built 10-11 meters high and reinforced with supporting towers around the perimeter. The inner courtyard of the mosque was very spacious to allow thousands of people to pray congregationally comfortably.

Most amazing was the Malwiya Tower with its unique spiral design. This tower functioned not only as a place for the call to prayer, but also as a watchtower for city security and a place to observe the stars.

Samarra artists developed a new decorative technique called "luster" for ceramics, which produced a shiny metallic effect on tiles. They also created a beveled carving style on plaster that gave beautiful shadow effects on walls.

🎨 *Illustration: Workers and architects building the Malwiya Tower using wooden scaffolding and ropes. Some are carrying bricks, some are mixing mortar, and some are carving decorations on walls. Caliph Al-Mutawakkil watches from a distance. Setting: construction site with 9th-century building equipment. Dominant colors: brown wooden scaffolding, red brick, white mortar, blue worker robes. Mood: busy and productive. Composition: medium shot with construction activity as main focus.*

---

## 🌍 Modern impact

The design of Malwiya Tower influenced mosque architecture throughout the Islamic world. Its unique spiral shape was later adopted by various buildings, including the Ibn Tulun Mosque in Cairo built in 879 CE.

Samarra's urban planning system became a model for the development of other Islamic cities. The concept of integrating palaces, commercial areas, and religious centers in one city complex was copied in various places.

Unfortunately, in 892 CE the capital was moved back to Baghdad and Samarra began to be abandoned. The Mongol attack in the 13th century CE caused severe damage, and in 2005 there was a terrorist attack that damaged part of the reconstructed dome.

Today, the Samarra Archaeological Site has been designated as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2007. International teams use 3D scanning and photogrammetry technology to document and preserve the remaining structures.

🎨 *Illustration: Comparison of Malwiya Tower in its glory days (left) intact and magnificent, with current condition (right) partially damaged but still standing strong. There's a modern archaeological team with digital equipment documenting. Setting: split scene showing past and present. Dominant colors: golden glory days vs faded brown modern, blue modern technology. Mood: nostalgia and hope for preservation. Composition: split screen with clear temporal contrast.*

---

## 📖 Islam & Science

Islam teaches that Allah SWT is everywhere and we can face any direction to pray, although the qibla remains the special direction for prayer. The Quran states:

> وَلِلَّهِ الْمَشْرِقُ وَالْمَغْرِبُ ۚ فَأَيْنَمَا تُوَلُّوا فَثَمَّ وَجْهُ اللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ وَاسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ ﴿١١٥﴾
>
> *"And to Allah belongs the east and the west. So wherever you turn, there is the face of Allah. Indeed, Allah is all-Encompassing and Knowing." ﴾115﴿*
> — QS. Al-Baqarah (2): 115

The construction of Samarra Mosque shows how Muslims during the Abbasid period used cutting-edge knowledge and technology to build places of worship. They combined knowledge from various civilizations to create extraordinary architectural works.

The Samarra library stored manuscripts in various languages: Arabic, Persian, Greek, Syriac, and Sanskrit. This shows that Islam encouraged the pursuit of knowledge from any source, in accordance with the hadith of Prophet Muhammad SAW: "Seek knowledge even unto China."

The Malwiya Tower was also used for astronomical observation, which was an important part of Islamic science. Muslim scientists used star observations to determine prayer times, qibla direction, and the Islamic calendar.

🎨 *Illustration: A Muslim scientist in green robes using an astrolabe and other astronomical instruments at the top of Malwiya Tower to observe stars. Below him, the city of Samarra sparkles at night. There are manuscript scrolls and writing instruments beside him. Setting: top of tower on a starry night. Dominant colors: green scientist's robe, golden astronomical instruments, blue night, white stars. Mood: calm and full of scientific concentration. Composition: medium shot with beautiful night sky background.*
masjid-agung-samarra-menara-malwiya.children-id:
# Masjid Agung Samarra dan Menara Malwiya: Keajaiban Arsitektur Abbasiyah

## 🏛 Fun fact

Menara Malwiya di Samarra adalah menara masjid pertama di dunia yang berbentuk spiral! Tingginya 52 meter dengan jalur memutar yang memungkinkan orang naik ke puncak tanpa menggunakan tangga. Bahkan keledai bisa naik sampai ke atas karena jalurnya yang landai dan lebar.

Masjid Agung Samarra pernah menjadi masjid terbesar di dunia pada masanya, dengan luas 38.000 meter persegi. Itu sama dengan sekitar 5 lapangan sepak bola! Masjid ini bisa menampung puluhan ribu jamaah sekaligus untuk shalat berjamaah.

Kota Samarra sendiri dibangun dari nol oleh Khalifah Al-Mu'tasim pada tahun 836 M sebagai ibukota baru Kekhalifahan Abbasiyah. Nama "Samarra" berasal dari bahasa Arab "Surra man ra'a" yang artinya "gembira siapa yang melihatnya."

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Panorama Kota Samarra dari udara menampilkan kompleks masjid dengan Menara Malwiya yang spiral sebagai pusat perhatian. Kota terbentang di sepanjang Sungai Tigris dengan istana, taman, dan bangunan-bangunan megah. Setting: Irak abad ke-9 dengan langit biru cerah. Warna dominan: coklat batu bata, hijau taman, biru sungai, emas kubah. Suasana: megah dan menakjubkan. Komposisi: aerial view wide shot dengan Menara Malwiya sebagai focal point.*

---

## 🔬 Penjelasan

Pembangunan Masjid Agung Samarra dimulai pada tahun 848 M dan selesai pada 852 M di bawah perintah Khalifah Al-Mutawakkil. Para arsitek menggunakan teknik konstruksi yang sangat maju, menggabungkan batu bata yang dibakar dengan mortar kapur berkualitas tinggi.

Dinding masjid dibangun setinggi 10-11 meter dan diperkuat dengan menara-menara penopang di sepanjang keliling. Halaman dalam masjid sangat luas untuk memungkinkan ribuan orang shalat berjamaah dengan nyaman.

Yang paling menakjubkan adalah Menara Malwiya dengan desain spiralnya yang unik. Menara ini tidak hanya berfungsi sebagai tempat adzan, tetapi juga sebagai menara pengawas untuk keamanan kota dan tempat untuk mengamati bintang-bintang.

Para seniman Samarra mengembangkan teknik dekorasi baru yang disebut "luster" untuk keramik, yang menghasilkan efek berkilau seperti logam pada ubin. Mereka juga menciptakan gaya ukiran miring pada plester yang memberikan efek bayangan indah pada dinding.

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Para pekerja dan arsitek sedang membangun Menara Malwiya dengan menggunakan perancah kayu dan tali. Ada yang mengangkut batu bata, ada yang mencampur mortar, dan ada yang mengukir hiasan di dinding. Khalifah Al-Mutawakkil mengawasi dari kejauhan. Setting: lokasi konstruksi dengan peralatan bangunan abad ke-9. Warna dominan: coklat kayu perancah, merah batu bata, putih mortar, biru jubah pekerja. Suasana: sibuk dan produktif. Komposisi: medium shot dengan aktivitas konstruksi sebagai fokus utama.*

---

## 🌍 Dampak modern

Desain Menara Malwiya mempengaruhi arsitektur masjid di seluruh dunia Islam. Bentuk spiralnya yang unik kemudian diadopsi oleh berbagai bangunan, termasuk Masjid Ibn Tulun di Kairo yang dibangun tahun 879 M.

Sistem perencanaan kota Samarra menjadi model untuk pengembangan kota-kota Islam lainnya. Konsep mengintegrasikan istana, area komersial, dan pusat keagamaan dalam satu kompleks kota ditiru di berbagai tempat.

Sayangnya, pada tahun 892 M ibukota dipindahkan kembali ke Baghdad dan Samarra mulai ditinggalkan. Serangan Mongolia pada abad ke-13 M menyebabkan kerusakan parah, dan pada tahun 2005 terjadi serangan teroris yang merusak sebagian kubah yang telah direkonstruksi.

Saat ini, Situs Arkeologi Samarra telah ditetapkan sebagai Situs Warisan Dunia UNESCO pada tahun 2007. Tim internasional menggunakan teknologi 3D scanning dan fotogrametri untuk mendokumentasi dan melestarikan struktur yang tersisa.

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Perbandingan Menara Malwiya pada masa kejayaan (kiri) yang utuh dan megah, dengan kondisi saat ini (kanan) yang sebagian rusak namun masih berdiri kokoh. Ada tim arkeolog modern dengan peralatan digital sedang mendokumentasi. Setting: split scene menunjukkan masa lalu dan sekarang. Warna dominan: emas masa kejayaan vs coklat pudar modern, biru teknologi modern. Suasana: nostalgia dan harapan pelestarian. Komposisi: split screen dengan kontras temporal yang jelas.*

---

## 📖 Islam & Ilmu

Islam mengajarkan bahwa Allah SWT ada di mana-mana dan kita bisa menghadap ke arah mana pun untuk berdoa, meskipun kiblat tetap menjadi arah khusus untuk shalat. Al-Quran berfirman:

> وَلِلَّهِ الْمَشْرِقُ وَالْمَغْرِبُ ۚ فَأَيْنَمَا تُوَلُّوا فَثَمَّ وَجْهُ اللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ وَاسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ ﴿١١٥﴾
>
> *"Dan kepunyaan Allah-lah timur dan barat, maka ke manapun kamu menghadap di situlah wajah Allah. Sesungguhnya Allah Maha Luas lagi Maha Mengetahui." ﴾115﴿*
> — QS. Al-Baqarah (2): 115

Pembangunan Masjid Samarra menunjukkan bagaimana umat Islam pada masa Abbasiyah menggunakan ilmu pengetahuan dan teknologi terdepan untuk membangun tempat ibadah. Mereka menggabungkan pengetahuan dari berbagai peradaban untuk menciptakan karya arsitektur yang luar biasa.

Perpustakaan Samarra menyimpan manuskrip dalam berbagai bahasa: Arab, Persia, Yunani, Syriac, dan Sanskrit. Ini menunjukkan bahwa Islam mendorong pencarian ilmu dari mana pun sumbernya, sesuai dengan hadits Nabi Muhammad SAW: "Carilah ilmu sampai ke negeri Cina."

Menara Malwiya juga digunakan untuk pengamatan astronomi, yang merupakan bagian penting dari ilmu pengetahuan Islam. Para ilmuwan Muslim menggunakan pengamatan bintang untuk menentukan waktu shalat, arah kiblat, dan kalender Islam.

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Seorang ilmuwan Muslim dengan jubah hijau sedang menggunakan astrolabe dan alat astronomi lainnya di puncak Menara Malwiya untuk mengamati bintang-bintang. Di bawahnya terlihat kota Samarra yang berkilauan di malam hari. Ada gulungan manuskrip dan alat tulis di sampingnya. Setting: puncak menara pada malam berbintang. Warna dominan: hijau jubah ilmuwan, emas alat astronomi, biru malam, putih bintang. Suasana: tenang dan penuh konsentrasi ilmiah. Komposisi: medium shot dengan latar langit malam yang indah.*
masjid-agung-samarra-menara-malwiya.general-en:
# The Great Mosque of Samarra and Malwiya Tower: Marvel of Abbasid Architecture

## Background: Samarra as the New Capital

In 836 CE, Caliph **Al-Mu'tasim Billah** (833-842 CE) decided to move the capital of the Abbasid Caliphate from Baghdad to Samarra, a specially built city on the banks of the Tigris River, about 125 kilometers north of Baghdad.^1 This decision was motivated by increasing tensions between Baghdad residents and the Turkish troops (*Mamalik*) who were becoming increasingly dominant in the government.

Samarra, derived from the Arabic phrase "*Surra man ra'a*" (meaning "happy is he who sees it"), was designed as a model city that reflected the magnificence and power of the Abbasid Caliphate.^2 The city was built on an extraordinarily large scale, stretching 57 kilometers along the Tigris River.

The construction of Samarra involved the best architects, artists, and craftsmen from throughout the Islamic world, including from Persia, Central Asia, and Mesopotamia.^3 Caliph Al-Mu'tasim invested tremendously in this project, aiming to create a symbol of power that could rival the magnificence of Constantinople and impress envoys from various nations.

## Architectural Concepts and Urban Planning

### Revolutionary Urban Layout

Samarra was one of the most ambitious examples of Islamic urban planning in history. The city was divided into several major complexes: the caliph's palace, residential complexes for high officials, military areas, commercial districts, and of course religious complexes with the mosque as its center.^4

A sophisticated irrigation system was developed to support life in this desert city. Water canals from the Tigris River were directed through a complex system to supply water to various parts of the city, including palace gardens and residential areas.^5

Streets in Samarra were designed with extraordinary width, some reaching 50 meters, to facilitate military parades and religious processions. The city's architecture combined ancient Mesopotamian traditions with Islamic innovations that prioritized communal and religious functions.^6

### Persian and Mesopotamian Architectural Influences

Samarra's design was heavily influenced by Sassanid Persian architectural traditions, especially in the use of *iwan* (open spaces with arched roofs), domes, and geometric ornamentation.^7 However, Abbasid architects adapted these elements to Islamic architectural needs that prioritized qibla orientation and space for congregational prayer.

The construction techniques used combined fired brick (*ajur*) with high-quality lime mortar. Thick walls were designed to withstand extreme desert weather and provide good thermal insulation.^8

## The Great Mosque of Samarra: Design and Construction

### Monumental Dimensions and Scale

Construction of the Great Mosque of Samarra began in 848 CE under the leadership of Caliph **Al-Mutawakkil Ala Allah** (847-861 CE) and was completed in 852 CE.^9 The mosque was designed to be the largest in the world at that time, with a total area of 38,000 square meters.

The mosque complex had outer dimensions of 240 x 156 meters, with outer walls 10-11 meters high built from brick and reinforced with supporting towers (*buttresses*) along the perimeter.^10 The spacious *sahn* (inner courtyard) allowed tens of thousands of worshippers to perform congregational prayers.

### Innovations in Liturgical Design

The Samarra Mosque introduced several important innovations in mosque architecture. The *mihrab* (prayer niche) was made larger than usual and decorated with beautiful Quranic calligraphy and geometric ornamentation.^11

The *maqsura* (special caliph's area) was designed with a sophisticated security system, with separate access from the palace and layered guards. This space reflected the evolution of the caliphate institution that was becoming increasingly formal and hierarchical.^12

The *riwaq* (colonnade with pillars) surrounded the inner courtyard with architectural style that combined Arab, Persian, and Mesopotamian elements. Brick pillars were reinforced with plaster and decorated with geometric motifs and calligraphy.^13

## Malwiya Tower: Architectural Marvel

### Unique Spiral Design

The Malwiya Tower (from the Arabic word "*malwiya*" meaning "spiral" or "twisting") is the most iconic feature of the Samarra Mosque. This 52-meter-high tower has a unique spiral design, with a winding ascending path that allows access to the top without using internal stairs.^14

The tower structure consists of several levels that narrow upward, with a total of seven spiral levels. The base diameter of the tower is 33 meters, gradually narrowing to reach 6 meters at the top.^15 This design is not only artistic but also practical, providing extraordinary structural stability.

### Construction Techniques and Materials

The tower was built using construction techniques that were very advanced for its time. The tower's core was made of rubble concrete (mixture of stone and mortar), covered with high-quality fired brick.^16 An internal drainage system was designed to prevent water damage from rain.

The external spiral path was protected by a safety wall about 1 meter high, allowing people to climb safely. The slope of the path was designed so that even pack animals like donkeys could climb to the top of the tower.^17

### Function and Symbolism

Although technically a mosque tower (*manara*), the Malwiya Tower had more complex functions than just a place for the call to prayer. The tower functioned as a watchtower for city security, a place for astronomical observation, and a symbol of Abbasid political power.^18

The spiral design of the tower was associated with Islamic cosmological traditions that view the universe as a layered structure connecting earth to heaven. Ascending to the top of the tower symbolized a spiritual journey toward closeness to Allah.^19

> وَلِلَّهِ الْمَشْرِقُ وَالْمَغْرِبُ ۚ فَأَيْنَمَا تُوَلُّوا فَثَمَّ وَجْهُ اللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ وَاسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ ﴿١١٥﴾
>
> *"And to Allah belongs the east and the west. So wherever you turn, there is the face of Allah. Indeed, Allah is all-Encompassing and Knowing." ﴾115﴿*
> — QS. Al-Baqarah (2): 115^20

## Ornamentation and Decorative Arts

### Evolution of Abbasid Islamic Art

The Samarra Mosque marked an important development in the evolution of Islamic art. The ornamentation used combined Arabic calligraphy, geometric motifs, and plant stylization (*tawriq*) that became characteristic of Abbasid Islamic art.^21

Quranic calligraphy was used extensively as a decorative element, especially in the form of elegant and proportional Kufic script. The verses chosen generally related to Allah's magnificence, the importance of congregational prayer, and the obligation to do good.^22

### Applied Arts Techniques

Samarra artists developed *luster* technique for ceramics that produced a shiny metallic effect on tile surfaces. This technique later spread throughout the Islamic world and became characteristic of medieval Islamic ceramics.^23

Decorative motifs in Samarra also introduced the *beveled* (slanted carving) style on plaster, which created shadow effects and depth on walls. This technique influenced the development of Islamic decorative arts in various regions.^24

## Religious Life Complex

### Liturgical and Social Activities

The Samarra Mosque functioned not only as a place of worship, but also as a center of intellectual and social life. The mosque complex included *maktab* (schools), *khizanah* (libraries), and rooms for *halaqah* (scholarly discussions).^25

Regular *majlis* (gatherings) were held in the mosque, discussing various religious, legal, and social issues. Caliphs often led theological discussions attended by scholars from various schools and streams of thought.^26

The *waqf* (endowment) system developed in Samarra became a model for financing religious institutions throughout the Islamic world. Revenue from waqf lands was used to maintain mosques, pay imams and muezzins, and provide assistance to the poor.^27

### Tolerance and Interreligious Dialogue

Interestingly, although Samarra was the center of the Islamic caliphate, the city also became a place for interreligious dialogue. **Caliph Al-Ma'mun** and his successors often invited Christian, Jewish, and Zoroastrian figures for theological discussions in the palace complex.^28

The Samarra library stored manuscripts in various languages: Arabic, Persian, Greek, Syriac, and Sanskrit. The translation tradition that began at Baitul Hikmah Baghdad was continued and expanded in Samarra.^29

## Decline and Damage

### Return of Capital to Baghdad

In 892 CE, Caliph **Al-Mu'tamid** decided to move the capital back to Baghdad, ending Samarra's era as the center of the caliphate.^30 This decision was motivated by extremely high maintenance costs and increasing political instability.

The capital's move caused Samarra to begin being gradually abandoned. Many residents moved following the government, leaving magnificent buildings without adequate care.^31

### Damage from War and Natural Disasters

Over the centuries, the Samarra Mosque suffered damage from various factors: earthquakes, seasonal flooding of the Tigris River, and war damage. The Mongol invasion in the 13th century CE caused very severe damage to the mosque structure.^32

In 2005, a terrorist attack damaged part of the golden dome that had been reconstructed in the modern period. This incident showed how vulnerable historical architectural heritage is amid contemporary conflicts.^33

## Architectural Influence and Legacy

### Influence on Mosque Architecture

The design of the Samarra Mosque, especially the concept of mosques with spacious courtyards and spiral towers, influenced mosque architecture in various Islamic regions. The Ibn Tulun Mosque in Cairo (879 CE) shows direct influence from the Samarra style.^34

Construction techniques and ornamentation developed in Samarra spread to Persia, Central Asia, and North Africa through the movement of architects and artists. The "Abbasid Samarra" style became one of the important references in Islamic architectural history.^35

### Legacy in Urban Planning

Samarra's urban planning concept influenced the development of subsequent Islamic cities. Integration between palace complexes, commercial areas, and religious centers became a model for cities like Fatimid Cairo and Umayyad Cordoba.^36

### Symbolism and Islamic Identity

The Malwiya Tower became one of the most recognized icons of Islamic architecture in the world. Its unique spiral design has inspired various interpretations in modern architecture, from contemporary mosque towers to secular buildings.^37

## Preservation and Restoration Efforts

### UNESCO World Heritage Status

In 2007, the Samarra Archaeological Site was designated as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, recognizing the outstanding universal value of this architectural complex.^38 However, the unstable security situation in Iraq makes preservation efforts very challenging.

### Digital Documentation Projects

Various international institutions have conducted digital documentation projects to preserve Samarra's architectural data. 3D scanning and photogrammetry technology are used to create accurate virtual reconstructions.^39

International archaeological teams work with the Iraqi government to develop long-term conservation strategies. The main focus is stabilizing existing structures and preventing further damage.^40

## Modern Lessons and Wisdom

### Importance of Comprehensive Urban Planning

Samarra shows the importance of comprehensive urban planning that integrates political, economic, social, and religious aspects. Although this project ultimately was not sustainable, its concepts remain relevant for modern urban development.^41

### Balance Between Ambition and Sustainability

Samarra's failure as a permanent capital provides lessons about the importance of balance between architectural ambition and long-term economic and social considerations. Grand projects will not last without sustainable system support.^42

### Heritage as Civilizational Identity

The Samarra Mosque and Malwiya Tower remain important symbols of Islamic civilization's glory. Preserving this architectural heritage is not only an archaeological responsibility, but also an obligation in maintaining civilizational identity for future generations.^43

---

## Bibliography

2. Herzfeld, Ernst, *Geschichte der Stadt Samarra*, Hamburg: Eckardt & Messtorff, 1948.
3. Creswell, K. A. C., *Early Muslim Architecture*, Volume 2, Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1940.

5. Adams, Robert McC., *Land Behind Baghdad*, Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1965.

7. Hillenbrand, Robert, *Islamic Architecture*, Edinburgh: Edinburgh University Press, 1994.
8. Bloom, Jonathan M., *Minaret: Symbol of Islam*, Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2013.
9. Al-Tabari, *Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk*, 15 vols., Leiden: Brill, 1879-1901.
10. Sarre, Friedrich, und Ernst Herzfeld, *Archäologische Reise im Euphrat- und Tigris-Gebiet*, 4 vols., Berlin: Dietrich Reimer, 1911-1920.
11. Sourdel-Thomine, Janine, "La mosquée et le palais dans l'art musulman primitif", *Cahiers de Civilisation Médiévale*, 7/3 (1964): 329-346.
12. Kennedy, Hugh, *The Court of the Caliphs*, London: Weidenfeld & Nicolson, 2004.
13. Ettinghausen, Richard, dan Oleg Grabar, *The Art and Architecture of Islam*, New Haven: Yale University Press, 1987.
14. Rogers, J. M., "Samarra: a Study in Medieval Town-Planning", *The Islamic City*, ed. A. H. Hourani dan S. M. Stern, Oxford: Bruno Cassirer, 1970.
15. Finster, Barbara, "Die Mosaiken der Umayyadenmoschee von Damaskus", *Kunst des Orients*, 7/2 (1970-71): 83-141.
16. Watson, Oliver, *Ceramics from Islamic Lands*, New York: Thames & Hudson, 2004.
17. Flood, Finbarr Barry, *The Great Mosque of Damascus*, Leiden: Brill, 2001.
