# Pertempuran Qadisiyyah — Penaklukan Persia (636 M)

## Konteks: Dua Dunia yang Berbenturan

Pada pertengahan abad ke-7 Masehi, Kekaisaran Persia Sasanid telah berdiri selama lebih dari empat abad. Dari ibu kotanya yang megah, Ktesifon (*al-Mada'in*), di tepi Sungai Tigris, dinasti Sasanid menguasai wilayah yang membentang dari Mesopotamia hingga perbatasan India. Tentara mereka termasuk yang terkuat di dunia — dilengkapi dengan kavaleri berat, gajah perang, dan tradisi militer yang telah diasah selama berabad-abad.^1 ^3

Namun di balik kemegahan itu, kekaisaran sedang rapuh. Al-Tabari dalam *Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk* mencatat bahwa dalam dua dekade sebelum Pertempuran Qadisiyyah, Persia telah mengalami lebih dari sepuluh pergantian raja — sebuah ketidakstabilan yang belum pernah terjadi sebelumnya.^1 Perang berkepanjangan dengan Bizantium (602-628 M) telah menguras sumber daya dan melemahkan moral tentara. Raja terakhir yang berkuasa dengan stabil, Khosrau II, terbunuh dalam kudeta, dan setelahnya kerajaan bergolak dalam perebutan kekuasaan.

Di sisi lain, kekuatan baru sedang bangkit dari padang pasir Arabia. Pasukan muslim di bawah kekhalifahan Umar ibn al-Khattab telah mulai memasuki wilayah perbatasan Irak. Bentrokan-bentrokan awal — seperti Pertempuran Jembatan (*Yawm al-Jisr*) pada tahun 634 M, di mana pasukan muslim mengalami kekalahan — menunjukkan bahwa menaklukkan Persia bukanlah perkara mudah.^1 ^2

Khalifah Umar memutuskan untuk mengirim pasukan besar di bawah komando **Sa'ad bin Abi Waqqash** — seorang sahabat senior yang termasuk dalam *al-'Asyarah al-Mubasysyarah* (sepuluh sahabat yang dijamin masuk surga) dan sepupu Nabi Muhammad ﷺ dari pihak ibu. Al-Tabari mencatat bahwa Umar memilih Sa'ad karena kombinasi keimanan, pengalaman militer, dan kematangan kepemimpinannya.^1

## Peristiwa: Empat Hari yang Mengguncang Kekaisaran

### Persiapan dan Diplomasi

Sebelum pertempuran dimulai, terjadi pertukaran diplomatik yang legendaris. Al-Tabari dan al-Baladhuri sama-sama meriwayatkan bahwa Sa'ad mengirim beberapa utusan ke istana **Rustam Farrukhzad**, panglima besar Persia.^1 ^2

Utusan pertama adalah **Rib'i bin Amir**, seorang Badui sederhana. Ketika ia memasuki kemah Rustam yang dipenuhi permadani sutra dan bantal emas, Rib'i tidak menunjukkan sedikit pun rasa gentar. Ia menancapkan tombaknya ke permadani, merobeknya, lalu duduk di tanah. Ketika Rustam bertanya apa tujuan kedatangan mereka, Rib'i menjawab dengan kalimat yang terkenal dalam sejarah:^1 ^2

*"Allah telah mengutus kami untuk mengeluarkan siapa saja yang Ia kehendaki dari penyembahan kepada hamba menuju penyembahan kepada Allah, dari kesempitan dunia menuju keluasannya, dan dari kezaliman agama-agama menuju keadilan Islam."*

Rustam menawarkan negosiasi tiga hari, berharap bisa menghindari pertempuran. Namun ketika diplomasi gagal, kedua pasukan bersiap untuk perang.

### Hari Pertama: Yawm Armath (Hari Kegelisahan)

Pertempuran Qadisiyyah berlangsung di dataran Qadisiyyah, dekat kota Hira di Irak, pada bulan Muharram 15 H (636 M). Al-Tabari mencatat bahwa pasukan muslim berjumlah sekitar 30.000-36.000, sementara pasukan Persia di bawah Rustam berjumlah antara 60.000 hingga lebih dari 100.000 — meskipun angka pasti masih diperdebatkan oleh sejarawan.^1 ^3

Hari pertama ditandai oleh serangan gajah perang Persia yang membuat kuda-kuda Arab panik dan lari. Al-Tabari meriwayatkan bahwa pasukan muslim belum pernah menghadapi gajah perang sebelumnya, dan dampak psikologisnya sangat besar.^1 Namun perlawanan gigih dari pasukan infanteri muslim berhasil menahan garis pertahanan.

### Hari Kedua: Yawm Aghwath (Hari Pertolongan)

Pada hari kedua, pasukan bantuan dari Syam — yang dikirim oleh Abu Ubaidah bin al-Jarrah atas perintah Umar — tiba di medan perang. Al-Baladhuri mencatat bahwa kedatangan pasukan segar ini sangat meningkatkan moral tentara muslim.^2 Pada hari ini pula, para pejuang muslim mulai menemukan cara melawan gajah perang — mereka menyerang mata dan belalai gajah dengan tombak, membuat binatang itu mengamuk dan justru menginjak-injak pasukan Persia sendiri.^1

### Hari Ketiga: Yawm 'Imas (Hari Keteguhan)

Hari ketiga adalah yang paling berdarah. Pertempuran berlangsung tanpa henti dari pagi hingga malam, dan berlanjut hingga malam hari — sesuatu yang sangat tidak lazim dalam tradisi peperangan Arab. Al-Tabari menyebut malam itu sebagai *Laylat al-Harir* (Malam Deru), karena suara pertempuran bagaikan deru binatang buas yang tak henti-hentinya.^1 ^3

Ibn al-Athir dalam *Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh* menggambarkan bahwa di malam itu, kedua pasukan bertarung dalam kegelapan dengan pedang dan tombak, nyaris tidak bisa membedakan kawan dari lawan kecuali melalui teriakan takbir yang berkumandang dari barisan muslim.^3

### Hari Keempat: Yawm al-Qadisiyyah (Hari Kemenangan)

Pada fajar hari keempat, badai pasir bertiup dari arah barat — menghantam wajah pasukan Persia. Al-Tabari mencatat bahwa angin ini memberikan keuntungan besar bagi pasukan muslim.^1 Dalam serangan terakhir yang menentukan, pasukan muslim berhasil menembus pertahanan pusat Persia.

**Rustam Farrukhzad** sendiri terbunuh — menurut riwayat al-Tabari, ia ditemukan bersembunyi di balik keledai beban ketika tentara muslim bernama Hilal bin Ullafah menemukannya dan membunuhnya.^1 Kematian panglima besar mereka menghancurkan semangat pasukan Persia, yang kemudian melarikan diri secara massal. Ribuan tentara Persia tenggelam saat berusaha menyeberangi Kanal Atiq dalam kepanikan.^1 ^3

## Dialog dan Dalil: Suara dari Medan Perang

Selain kata-kata Rib'i bin Amir yang telah disebutkan, al-Tabari mencatat beberapa momen penting lainnya:

Ketika Sa'ad bin Abi Waqqash — yang saat itu sedang sakit bisul sehingga tidak bisa menunggang kuda — memimpin pertempuran dari atas menara, ia memotivasi pasukannya dengan mengutip firman Allah:^1

> وَلَقَدْ كَتَبْنَا فِي الزَّبُورِ مِنْ بَعْدِ الذِّكْرِ أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُهَا عِبَادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ ﴿١٠٥﴾
>
> *"Dan sungguh, telah Kami tulis di dalam Zabur sesudah (tertulis dalam) al-Lauh al-Mahfuzh, bahwa bumi ini akan diwarisi oleh hamba-hamba-Ku yang saleh. ﴾105﴿"*
> — QS. Al-Anbiya (21): 105

Al-Baladhuri juga mencatat pesan Umar kepada Sa'ad sebelum keberangkatan:^2

*"Wahai Sa'ad, janganlah engkau terpedaya oleh kenyataan bahwa engkau disebut paman Rasulullah. Sesungguhnya Allah tidak menghapus kejahatan dengan kejahatan, tetapi menghapus kejahatan dengan kebaikan. Sesungguhnya tidak ada hubungan antara Allah dan siapa pun kecuali melalui ketaatan. "*

## Analisis dan Hikmah: Runtuhnya Sebuah Adidaya

### Kemenangan Keyakinan atas Kekuatan Material

Pertempuran Qadisiyyah adalah salah satu contoh paling dramatis dalam sejarah tentang bagaimana keyakinan dan moral dapat mengalahkan keunggulan material. Pasukan Persia memiliki segalanya — jumlah yang lebih besar, persenjataan lebih baik, gajah perang, dan tradisi militer berabad-abad. Pasukan muslim hanya membawa iman, disiplin, dan kesediaan untuk mati di jalan Allah.

Al-Tabari mencatat bahwa banyak pejuang muslim yang datang ke Qadisiyyah dengan perlengkapan seadanya — beberapa bahkan harus berbagi pedang.^1 Namun apa yang mereka miliki, dan tidak dimiliki oleh pasukan Persia, adalah tujuan yang lebih tinggi dari sekadar harta rampasan atau kesetiaan kepada raja.

### Diplomasi Sebelum Pedang

Fakta bahwa Sa'ad mengirim beberapa utusan sebelum pertempuran menunjukkan prinsip penting dalam etika perang Islam: perang adalah pilihan terakhir. Setiap penduduk wilayah yang dihampiri pasukan muslim diberikan tiga pilihan — memeluk Islam, membayar *jizyah* dan hidup damai, atau berperang. Prinsip ini jauh lebih manusiawi dibandingkan praktik peperangan yang umum pada masa itu.^1 ^2

### Jatuhnya Kekaisaran — Pelajaran tentang Keangkuhan

Respons Rustam dan para bangsawan Persia terhadap utusan muslim — campuran antara penghinaan dan keheranan melihat "orang-orang Badui miskin" yang berani menantang kekaisaran terbesar di dunia — mencerminkan keangkuhan yang menjadi benih kehancuran mereka sendiri. Ibn al-Athir mencatat bahwa para bangsawan Persia meremehkan pasukan muslim karena penampilan mereka yang sederhana, tidak menyadari bahwa kekuatan sebuah pasukan tidak terletak pada kemewahan, melainkan pada keberanian dan tujuan.^3

### Dampak Sejarah Jangka Panjang

Pertempuran Qadisiyyah bukan sekadar kemenangan militer — ia menandai awal dari perubahan peradaban. Setelah Qadisiyyah, Ktesifon jatuh, dan seluruh Mesopotamia berada di bawah kekuasaan Islam. Dalam beberapa dekade, Islam menyebar ke seluruh wilayah bekas Kekaisaran Sasanid, dan budaya Persia — alih-alih musnah — justru bertransformasi dan berkontribusi besar pada peradaban Islam. Ilmuwan, penyair, dan pemikir Persia kelak menjadi pilar penting *Zaman Keemasan Islam*.^1 ^3

## Daftar Pustaka

1. **Al-Tabari, Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Jarir.** *Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk* (Sejarah Para Rasul dan Raja-Raja). Jilid 3-4. Beirut: Dar al-Turats, 1387 H.

2. **Al-Baladhuri, Ahmad ibn Yahya.** *Futuh al-Buldan* (Penaklukan Negeri-Negeri). Beirut: Dar al-Kutub al-Ilmiyyah, 1988.

3. **Ibn al-Athir, Izz al-Din.** *Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh* (Sejarah Lengkap). Jilid 2. Beirut: Dar al-Kitab al-Arabi, 1997.

4. Al-Qur'an al-Karim. QS. Al-Anbiya (21): 105.

penaklukan-yerusalem-al-quds.children-en:
# The Conquest of Jerusalem 🕌

---

## A Very Special Holy City

**Jerusalem** — called **Al-Quds** in Arabic — is a very special city. It is holy to three religions: Islam, Christianity, and Judaism. In this city stands **Al-Aqsa Mosque**, from where Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was taken up to the heavens during the miraculous Night Journey.

In 637 CE, Jerusalem was ruled by the Byzantine Empire (Eastern Rome). After the Muslim armies won great battles across Syria, the time came to liberate Jerusalem.

The Muslim commander, **Abu Ubaidah ibn al-Jarrah**, surrounded the city. But the Muslim army did not attack — they waited patiently. The Christian leader of Jerusalem, **Patriarch Sophronius**, finally agreed to surrender the city — with one condition: **"I will only surrender to the caliph himself!"**

🎨 *Illustration: A view of Jerusalem from a distance, with domes and grand stone walls. In front of the city, Muslim soldiers camped in orderly rows. A sunset sky in orange and purple. Dominant colors: golden stone, purple, orange. Mood: hopeful and grand. Composition: city in background, camp in foreground.*

---

## The Caliph Who Walked on Foot

When he heard Sophronius's condition, Caliph **Umar ibn al-Khattab** decided to travel from Madinah to Jerusalem himself. But the way he traveled surprised everyone!

Umar brought only **one camel and one servant**. And they **took turns** riding the camel — sometimes Umar rode, sometimes his servant did! When they arrived near Jerusalem, it happened to be the servant's turn on the camel, and Umar was walking through the mud on foot.

General Abu Ubaidah was embarrassed to see the caliph arrive like that. He said: "O Commander of the Faithful, the people of the city will see you this way..."

Umar answered firmly: **"We are a people whom Allah honored through Islam. If we seek honor from anything else, Allah will humiliate us."**

The people of Jerusalem, who had been afraid, were now amazed — the ruler of half the world turned out to be a truly humble man!

🎨 *Illustration: A muddy road leading to a great city gate. A man in a simple robe (Umar) walks barefoot through the mud, leading a camel ridden by his servant. In the distance, the gate of Jerusalem stands open. People watch with astonished faces. Dominant colors: earthy brown, blue sky, white stone. Mood: humble and awe-inspiring. Composition: Umar in center walking toward the gate in background.*

---

## A Promise of Protection for Everyone

After arriving, Umar gave the people of Jerusalem a very important **written promise**. It was called the **Covenant of Aelia**, and it said:

- Churches **must not** be destroyed or taken
- Christian residents are **safe** — their lives and property are protected
- **No one** will be forced to change their religion
- Everyone will be treated **fairly**

Then a truly historic moment happened. When prayer time came, Umar and Sophronius were inside the **Church of the Holy Sepulchre** — the holiest church for Christians. Sophronius invited Umar to pray inside.

But Umar **refused**! He said: "If I pray here, Muslims after me will take this church and turn it into a mosque." So Umar prayed **outside the church** — to protect the rights of Christians!

🎨 *Illustration: In front of a grand church with massive wooden doors. Umar spreads a simple prayer mat on the ground outside, prostrating. Sophronius stands at the church door, watching with a face full of respect. Sunlight shining on both of them. Dominant colors: warm gold, cream stone, blue sky. Mood: peaceful and respectful. Composition: Umar prostrating on the left, Sophronius at the church door on the right.*

---

## Cleaning Al-Aqsa Mosque

Umar then searched for the site of **Al-Aqsa Mosque** — the holy place mentioned in the Quran. But he found a sad sight: the area had been turned into a **rubbish dump** by the Roman rulers!

Without hesitation, Umar immediately got down on his hands and knees and **started cleaning it himself**. He picked up rubbish and dirt with his own hands. Seeing the caliph at work, the companions rushed to help. Together, they cleaned the sacred area.

This was where a simple mosque was later built — the beginning of the Al-Aqsa Mosque we know today.

Umar also did something very fair: he allowed **Jews to return and live in Jerusalem** — after being expelled by the Romans for hundreds of years. So the Muslim conquest of Jerusalem brought good to everyone — Muslims, Christians, and Jews alike!

🎨 *Illustration: A wide open area being cleaned up. Umar kneeling and picking up rubble with his hands, surrounded by companions working alongside him. In the background, a bright blue sky and the silhouette of Jerusalem. Dominant colors: bright blue, gold, earthy brown. Mood: spirited and full of new hope. Composition: Umar in center foreground, companions around him, city skyline in background.*
penaklukan-yerusalem-al-quds.children-id:
# Penaklukan Yerusalem 🕌

---

## Kota Suci yang Istimewa

**Yerusalem** — atau dalam bahasa Arab disebut **Al-Quds** — adalah kota yang sangat istimewa. Kota ini suci bagi tiga agama: Islam, Kristen, dan Yahudi. Di kota inilah terdapat **Masjidil Aqsha**, tempat Nabi Muhammad ﷺ diangkat ke langit dalam peristiwa Isra' Mi'raj.

Pada tahun 637 M, Yerusalem dikuasai oleh Kerajaan Bizantium (Romawi Timur). Setelah pasukan muslim memenangkan pertempuran-pertempuran besar di Syam, tibalah saatnya Yerusalem dibebaskan.

Panglima muslim, **Abu Ubaidah bin al-Jarrah**, mengepung kota ini. Tapi pasukan muslim tidak menyerang — mereka menunggu dengan sabar. Pemimpin Kristen Yerusalem, **Patriark Sophronius**, akhirnya setuju menyerahkan kota — dengan satu syarat: **"Aku hanya mau menyerah kepada khalifah sendiri!"**

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Pemandangan kota Yerusalem dari kejauhan, dengan kubah-kubah dan tembok batu yang megah. Di depan kota, pasukan muslim berkemah dengan tertib. Langit senja berwarna jingga dan ungu. Warna dominan: batu emas, ungu, jingga. Mood: penuh harap dan agung. Komposisi: kota di latar belakang, perkemahan di depan.*

---

## Khalifah yang Berjalan Kaki

Ketika mendengar syarat Sophronius, Khalifah **Umar ibn al-Khattab** memutuskan untuk pergi sendiri dari Madinah ke Yerusalem. Tapi caranya pergi membuat semua orang terkejut!

Umar hanya membawa **satu ekor unta dan satu orang pelayan**. Dan mereka **bergantian** naik unta — kadang Umar yang naik, kadang pelayannya! Ketika sampai di dekat Yerusalem, kebetulan giliran pelayan yang naik unta, dan Umar berjalan kaki di lumpur.

Jenderal Abu Ubaidah malu melihat khalifah datang dalam keadaan seperti itu. Ia berkata: "Wahai Amirul Mukminin, penduduk kota akan melihatmu begini.."

Umar menjawab dengan tegas: **"Kita adalah kaum yang dimuliakan Allah dengan Islam. Jika kita mencari kemuliaan dari selain itu, Allah akan menghinakan kita."**

Penduduk Yerusalem yang tadinya takut justru terkagum — pemimpin yang menguasai setengah dunia ternyata orang yang sangat rendah hati!

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Jalan berlumpur menuju gerbang kota besar. Seorang pria berjubah sederhana (Umar) berjalan kaki di lumpur, menuntun unta yang ditunggangi pelayannya. Di kejauhan, gerbang kota Yerusalem terbuka. Orang-orang berdiri menonton dengan wajah takjub. Warna dominan: cokelat tabiru langit, putih batu. Mood: rendah hati dan mengagumkan. Komposisi: Umar di tengah berjalan menuju gerbang di latar belakang.*

---

## Janji Perlindungan untuk Semua

Setelah tiba, Umar memberikan **perjanjian tertulis** yang sangat penting kepada penduduk Yerusalem. Perjanjian ini disebut **Perjanjian Aelia**, dan isinya luar biasa:

- Gereja-gereja **tidak boleh** dihancurkan atau diambil
- Penduduk Kristen **aman** — jiwa dan harta mereka dilindungi
- **Tidak ada** yang dipaksa pindah agama
- Semua orang diperlakukan **adil**

Kemudian terjadi momen yang sangat bersejarah. Ketika waktu salat tiba, Umar dan Sophronius sedang berada di dalam **Gereja Kebangkitan** — gereja paling suci bagi umat Kristen. Sophronius mempersilakan Umar salat di dalam.

Tapi Umar **menolak**! Ia berkata: "Kalau aku salat di sini, nanti umat muslim setelahku akan mengambil gereja ini dan mengubahnya jadi masjid." Jadi Umar salat **di luar gereja** — demi melindungi hak umat Kristen!

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Di depan gereja besar dengan pintu kayu raksasa. Umar menggelar sajadah sederhana di tanah di luar gereja, bersujud. Sophronius berdiri di pintu gereja, menyaksikan dengan wajah penuh hormat. Cahaya matahari menyinari keduanya. Warna dominan: emas hangat, batu krem, biru langit. Mood: damai dan penuh penghormatan. Komposisi: Umar sujud di kiri, Sophronius di pintu gereja kanan.*

---

## Membersihkan Masjidil Aqsha

Umar kemudian mencari lokasi **Masjidil Aqsha** — tempat suci yang disebutkan dalam al-Quran. Tapi ia menemukan pemandangan yang menyedihkan: area itu telah dijadikan **tempat pembuangan sampah** oleh penguasa Romawi!

Tanpa ragu, Umar langsung **turun tangan membersihkannya sendiri**. Ia mengangkat sampah dan kotoran dengan tangannya sendiri. Melihat khalifah bekerja, para sahabat segera ikut membantu. Bersama-sama, mereka membersihkan area suci itu.

Di tempat inilah kemudian dibangun masjid sederhana — cikal bakal Masjidil Aqsha yang kita kenal sekarang.

Umar juga melakukan hal yang sangat adil: ia mengizinkan **kaum Yahudi kembali tinggal di Yerusalem** — setelah ratusan tahun diusir oleh penguasa Romawi. Jadi penaklukan Yerusalem oleh umat Islam membawa kebaikan untuk semua orang — muslim, Kristen, dan Yahudi!

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Area terbuka luas yang sedang dibersihkan. Umar berlutut mengangkat puing dengan tangan, dikelilingi sahabat yang ikut bekerja. Di latar belakang, langit biru cerah dan siluet kota Yerusalem. Warna dominan: biru cerah, emas, cokelat tanah. Mood: penuh semangat dan harapan baru. Komposisi: Umar di tengah depan, sahabat di sekeliling, kota di latar belakang.*
penaklukan-yerusalem-al-quds.general-en:
# The Conquest of Jerusalem / Al-Quds (637 CE)

## Context: The Holy City of Three Faiths

Jerusalem — known in the Islamic tradition as *al-Quds* (The Holy) or *Iliya'* (from the Roman name *Aelia Capitolina*) — was no ordinary city. It was sacred to three great religions: Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. For Muslims, Jerusalem held a special station as the first *qiblah* before the direction of prayer was changed to the Ka'bah, and as the place from which the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ ascended to the heavens during the *Isra' wa al-Mi'raj*.^1 ^2

The Quran immortalizes this sacred bond^4:

> سُبْحَانَ الَّذِي أَسْرَىٰ بِعَبْدِهِ لَيْلًا مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى الَّذِي بَارَكْنَا حَوْلَهُ ﴿١﴾
>
> *"Glory be to the One Who took His servant on a night journey from the Sacred Mosque to the Farthest Mosque, whose surroundings We have blessed. ﴾1﴿"*
> — QS. Al-Isra (17): 1

In 637 CE, Jerusalem was under Byzantine (Eastern Roman) rule. The city had been held by Byzantium for centuries, though it briefly fell to Sasanian Persia in 614 CE before being recaptured by Emperor Heraclius in 629 CE. Its population was predominantly Christian, with a Jewish community that had been expelled by Rome and only partially permitted to return.^1

After the Muslim victory at the Battle of Yarmuk (636 CE), which shattered the main Byzantine force in Syria, cities fell one by one to the Islamic armies. **Abu Ubaidah ibn al-Jarrah**, supreme commander of Muslim forces in Syria, besieged Jerusalem. Al-Tabari and al-Baladhuri record that the siege lasted several months without major violence — the Muslim army did not storm the city but waited patiently.^1 ^2

## Events: The Caliph's Arrival That Changed History

### Patriarch Sophronius: "I Will Surrender Only to the Caliph"

**Patriarch Sophronius**, the highest authority of the Orthodox Church in Jerusalem, recognized that military defense was no longer viable. Yet he set an unusual condition: he would surrender the city only to the caliph of the Muslims in person — not to any general.^1 ^2

Abu Ubaidah sent word to Caliph **Umar ibn al-Khattab** in Madinah, relaying the Patriarch's condition. Al-Tabari records that Umar consulted the companions. **Ali ibn Abi Talib** advised Umar to go himself, given Jerusalem's importance in the Islamic tradition.^1

### A Journey That Astonished the World

Umar set out from Madinah to Jerusalem with a remarkably humble retinue. Al-Baladhuri and Ibn Kathir relate the now-legendary details: Umar brought only **one camel and one servant**. The two took turns riding — when it was the servant's turn to ride, Umar walked on foot, leading the camel.^2 ^3

Ibn Kathir in *Al-Bidayah wa al-Nihayah* records that as the party neared Jerusalem, it happened to be the servant's turn on the camel while Umar walked barefoot, sandals in hand, through a muddy stretch. Abu Ubaidah, who had come out to meet him, felt embarrassed and said: "O Commander of the Faithful, the people of this city will see you like this..." Umar replied:^3

*"O Abu Ubaidah, had anyone other than you said that, I would have punished him. We are a people whom Allah honored through Islam. If we seek honor from anything else, Allah will humiliate us."*

### The Covenant of Aelia — *al-'Uhdah al-'Umariyyah*

When Umar arrived in Jerusalem, Patriarch Sophronius received him and handed over the keys of the city. Al-Tabari records that Umar then issued a written guarantee to the people of Jerusalem known as the **Covenant of Aelia** (*al-'Uhdah al-'Umariyyah*), one of the most important documents in the history of religious tolerance:^1

*"In the name of Allah, the Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate. This is the guarantee of safety given by the servant of Allah, Umar, Commander of the Faithful, to the people of Iliya' (Jerusalem). He grants them safety for their lives, their property, their churches, their crosses, their sick and their healthy, and for all their co-religionists. Their churches shall not be occupied, nor destroyed, nor diminished — neither the churches themselves, their grounds, their crosses, nor their property. They shall not be compelled to abandon their religion, and none of them shall be harmed."*

Al-Baladhuri adds that the covenant also required the people of Jerusalem to pay the *jizyah* (protection tax) as in other cities of Syria, and to harbor no Roman spies.^2

### Umar at Al-Aqsa Mosque

Al-Tabari and Ibn Kathir recount that after the covenant was signed, Sophronius took Umar on a tour of the holy city. When prayer time arrived and they were inside the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, Sophronius invited Umar to pray inside the church.^1 ^3

Umar gently declined. He said: *"Were I to pray here, the Muslims after me would seize this place and say: Umar prayed here. Then they would turn it into a mosque."* Umar instead prayed outside the church, a few steps from its entrance.^1 ^3 This decision — refusing to pray inside a church in order to protect Christian rights — became a symbol of tolerance remembered to this day.

Umar then searched for the site of **Al-Aqsa Mosque** — the sacred place mentioned in the Quran. He found that the area (now known as the Haram al-Sharif / Temple Mount) had been turned into a rubbish dump by the Roman authorities as a deliberate insult to Jewish tradition.^1 ^3 Umar himself began clearing the site with his own hands, and the companions followed. It was here that a simple mosque was later built — the precursor to Al-Aqsa Mosque.

## Key Sayings and Scriptural Evidence

Beyond the dialogues already mentioned, several other significant moments stand out:

When Umar beheld the splendor of Jerusalem and compared it with the simplicity of Madinah, he remembered Allah's promise and said, as recorded by al-Tabari:^1

*"All praise belongs to Allah, Who has honored Islam and the Muslims."*

Ibn Kathir also records that when Umar entered the neglected, filthy precinct of Al-Aqsa, he proclaimed the *takbir* and said: *"By Allah, this is the mosque that the Messenger of Allah ﷺ described to me."*^3

The Prophet ﷺ himself affirmed the special status of Al-Aqsa Mosque:

> لَا تُشَدُّ الرِّحَالُ إِلَّا إِلَى ثَلَاثَةِ مَسَاجِدَ: الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ، وَمَسْجِدِي هَذَا، وَالْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى
>
> *"No journey should be undertaken (for worship) except to three mosques: the Sacred Mosque, this mosque of mine (the Prophet's Mosque), and Al-Aqsa Mosque."*
> — Bukhari, no. 1189; Muslim, no. 1397

## Analysis and Lessons: A Model of Tolerance for All Time

### A Conquest Without Bloodshed

Umar's conquest of Jerusalem was one of the rare instances in history when a holy city changed hands **without bloodshed**. There was no massacre, no looting, no forced conversion. Compare this with the Crusader capture of Jerusalem in 1099 CE — marked by mass slaughter of Muslim, Jewish, and even Eastern Christian inhabitants.^1 ^2

### The Covenant of Aelia as a Charter of Rights

The Covenant of Aelia anticipated modern concepts of religious freedom and minority rights by over a millennium. It guaranteed:
- Safety of life and property for non-Muslim residents
- Preservation of churches and places of worship
- Freedom of religion without coercion
- Equal legal protection

Western historians, including Karen Armstrong in *Jerusalem: One City, Three Faiths*, acknowledge that the Islamic conquest of Jerusalem ushered in an era of religious tolerance far surpassing that of previous rulers.

### Simplicity as Strength

Umar's arrival on foot, in a patched robe, without a royal entourage, created a dramatic contrast with Byzantine splendor. Yet it was precisely this simplicity that impressed the people of Jerusalem — long accustomed to the opulence and oppression of Roman rulers — and convinced them that their new sovereign was a just man.^3

### Protecting Christian Rights

Umar's refusal to pray inside the Church of the Holy Sepulchre was more than a courtesy — it was legal precedent. By declining to pray there, Umar prevented any future Muslim claim to the church. This demonstrates the long-term vision of a leader who considered not only the present moment but the consequences of his decisions for generations to come.^1 ^3

### Restoring Jewish Rights

Al-Tabari records that after the conquest, Umar permitted Jews to return and reside in Jerusalem — a right that had been denied by Roman and Byzantine rulers for centuries.^1 This made the Islamic conquest a moment of liberation not only for Muslims, but also for the Jewish community long exiled from their holy city.

## Bibliography

1. **Al-Tabari, Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Jarir.** *Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk* (The History of Prophets and Kings). Vols. 3-4. Beirut: Dar al-Turath, 1387 AH.

2. **Al-Baladhuri, Ahmad ibn Yahya.** *Futuh al-Buldan* (The Conquest of Lands). Beirut: Dar al-Kutub al-Ilmiyyah, 1988.

3. **Ibn Kathir, Ismail ibn Umar.** *Al-Bidayah wa al-Nihayah* (The Beginning and the End). Vol. 7. Beirut: Dar al-Fikr, 1986.
4. Al-Qur'an al-Karim. QS. Al-Isra (17): 1.
penaklukan-yerusalem-al-quds.general-id:
# Penaklukan Yerusalem / Al-Quds (637 M)

## Konteks: Kota Suci Tiga Agama

Yerusalem — yang dalam tradisi Islam disebut *al-Quds* (Yang Suci) atau *Iliya'* (dari nama Romawi *Aelia Capitolina*) — bukanlah kota biasa. Ia adalah kota suci bagi tiga agama besar: Yahudi, Kristen, dan Islam. Bagi umat Islam, Yerusalem memiliki kedudukan istimewa sebagai kiblat pertama sebelum dialihkan ke Ka'bah, dan sebagai tempat Nabi Muhammad ﷺ diangkat ke langit dalam peristiwa *Isra' Mi'raj*.^1 ^2

Al-Quran mengabadikan hubungan sakral ini^4:

> سُبْحَانَ الَّذِي أَسْرَىٰ بِعَبْدِهِ لَيْلًا مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى الَّذِي بَارَكْنَا حَوْلَهُ ﴿١﴾
>
> *"Maha Suci Allah yang telah memperjalankan hamba-Nya pada suatu malam dari Masjidil Haram ke Masjidil Aqsha yang telah Kami berkahi sekelilingnya. ﴾1﴿"*
> — QS. Al-Isra (17): 1

Pada tahun 637 M, Yerusalem berada di bawah kekuasaan Bizantium (Romawi Timur). Kota ini telah dikuasai Bizantium selama berabad-abad, meskipun sempat jatuh ke tangan Persia Sasanid pada tahun 614 M sebelum direbut kembali oleh Kaisar Heraklius pada tahun 629 M. Penduduknya mayoritas Kristen, dengan komunitas Yahudi yang sempat diusir oleh Romawi dan hanya diizinkan kembali secara terbatas.^1

Setelah kemenangan muslim di Pertempuran Yarmuk (636 M) yang menghancurkan kekuatan utama Bizantium di Syam, satu per satu kota-kota besar jatuh ke tangan pasukan Islam. **Abu Ubaidah bin al-Jarrah**, panglima tertinggi pasukan muslim di Syam, mengepung Yerusalem. Al-Tabari dan al-Baladhuri mencatat bahwa pengepungan berlangsung selama beberapa bulan tanpa kekerasan besar — pasukan muslim tidak menyerang kota secara langsung, melainkan menunggu dengan sabar.^1 ^2

## Peristiwa: Kedatangan Khalifah yang Mengubah Sejarah

### Patriark Sophronius: "Aku Hanya Menyerah kepada Khalifah"

**Patriark Sophronius**, pemimpin tertinggi Gereja Ortodoks di Yerusalem, menyadari bahwa pertahanan militer tidak lagi memungkinkan. Namun ia mengajukan satu syarat yang tidak biasa: ia hanya bersedia menyerahkan kota kepada khalifah kaum muslimin secara langsung — bukan kepada jenderal mana pun.^1 ^2

Abu Ubaidah mengirim surat kepada Khalifah **Umar ibn al-Khattab** di Madinah, menyampaikan syarat Sophronius. Al-Tabari mencatat bahwa Umar bermusyawarah dengan para sahabat. **Ali bin Abi Thalib** menyarankan agar Umar pergi sendiri, mengingat pentingnya Yerusalem dalam tradisi Islam.^1

### Perjalanan yang Mengejutkan Dunia

Umar berangkat dari Madinah menuju Yerusalem dengan rombongan yang sangat sederhana. Al-Baladhuri dan Ibn Kathir meriwayatkan detail perjalanan yang menjadi legendaris: Umar hanya membawa **satu ekor unta dan seorang pelayan**. Keduanya bergantian menunggang unta — ketika tiba giliran pelayan naik, Umar berjalan kaki menuntun unta. ^2 ^3

Ibn Kathir dalam *Al-Bidayah wa al-Nihayah* mencatat bahwa ketika rombongan mendekati Yerusalem, kebetulan giliran pelayan yang menunggang unta, sementara Umar berjalan kaki dengan sandal di tangan karena sedang menyeberangi genangan air. Abu Ubaidah, yang menjemputnya, merasa malu dan berkata: "Wahai Amirul Mukminin, penduduk kota ini akan melihatmu dalam keadaan seperti ini..." Umar menjawab:^3

*"Wahai Abu Ubaidah, seandainya orang selain engkau yang berkata demikian, niscaya aku akan menghukumnya. Kita adalah kaum yang dimuliakan Allah dengan Islam. Jika kita mencari kemuliaan dari selain itu, niscaya Allah akan menghinakan kita."*

### Perjanjian Aelia — *al-'Uhdah al-'Umariyyah*

Ketika Umar tiba di Yerusalem, Patriark Sophronius menyambutnya dan menyerahkan kunci kota. Al-Tabari mencatat bahwa Umar kemudian memberikan perjanjian tertulis kepada penduduk Yerusalem yang dikenal sebagai **Perjanjian Aelia** (*al-'Uhdah al-'Umariyyah*), salah satu dokumen paling penting dalam sejarah toleransi beragama:^1

*"Bismillahirrahmanirrahim. Ini adalah jaminan keamanan yang diberikan oleh hamba Allah, Umar, Amirul Mukminin, kepada penduduk Iliya' (Yerusalem). Ia memberikan jaminan keamanan bagi jiwa mereka, harta mereka, gereja-gereja mereka, salib-salib mereka, orang sakit dan orang sehat di antara mereka, serta seluruh pemeluk agama mereka. Gereja-gereja mereka tidak akan ditempati, tidak akan dihancurkan, dan tidak akan dikurangi — baik gereja itu sendiri, halamannya, salib-salibnya, maupun harta mereka. Mereka tidak akan dipaksa meninggalkan agama mereka, dan tidak seorang pun dari mereka yang akan disakiti."*

Al-Baladhuri menambahkan bahwa perjanjian ini juga menetapkan kewajiban penduduk Yerusalem untuk membayar *jizyah* (pajak perlindungan) sebagaimana penduduk kota-kota lain di Syam, dan mereka tidak boleh menampung mata-mata Romawi.^2

### Umar di Masjidil Aqsha

Al-Tabari dan Ibn Kathir meriwayatkan bahwa setelah perjanjian ditandatangani, Sophronius mengajak Umar berkeliling kota suci. Ketika waktu salat tiba dan mereka sedang berada di dalam Gereja Kebangkitan (Church of the Holy Sepulchre), Sophronius mempersilakan Umar untuk salat di dalam gereja.^1 ^3

Umar menolak dengan halus. Ia berkata: *"Seandainya aku salat di sini, kaum muslimin setelahku akan mengambil tempat ini dan berkata: Umar pernah salat di sini. Maka mereka akan mengubahnya menjadi masjid."* Umar kemudian salat di luar gereja, beberapa langkah dari pintunya.^1 ^3 Keputusan ini — menolak salat di dalam gereja demi melindungi hak umat Kristen — menjadi simbol toleransi yang dikenang hingga kini.

Umar kemudian mencari lokasi **Masjidil Aqsha** — tempat suci yang disebutkan dalam al-Quran. Ia menemukan bahwa area tersebut (yang kini dikenal sebagai Haram al-Sharif / Temple Mount) telah dijadikan tempat pembuangan sampah oleh penguasa Romawi sebagai bentuk penghinaan terhadap tradisi Yahudi.^1 ^3 Umar sendiri mulai membersihkan area itu dengan tangannya, dan para sahabat mengikutinya. Di tempat inilah kemudian dibangun masjid sederhana yang menjadi cikal bakal Masjidil Aqsha.

## Dialog dan Dalil: Momen-Momen yang Menentukan

Selain dialog-dialog yang telah disebutkan, ada beberapa momen penting lainnya:

Ketika Umar melihat kemegahan Yerusalem dan membandingkannya dengan kesederhanaan Madinah, ia mengingat janji Allah dan berkata, sebagaimana dicatat oleh al-Tabari:^1

*"Segala puji bagi Allah yang telah memuliakan Islam dan kaum muslimin."*

Ibn Kathir juga mencatat bahwa ketika Umar memasuki area Masjidil Aqsha yang kotor dan terabaikan, ia mengucapkan takbir dan berkata: *"Demi Allah, inilah masjid yang diceritakan Rasulullah ﷺ kepadaku."*^3

Nabi Muhammad ﷺ sendiri telah menegaskan kedudukan istimewa Masjidil Aqsha:

> لَا تُشَدُّ الرِّحَالُ إِلَّا إِلَى ثَلَاثَةِ مَسَاجِدَ: الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ، وَمَسْجِدِي هَذَا، وَالْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى
>
> *"Tidak boleh melakukan perjalanan (ibadah) kecuali ke tiga masjid: Masjidil Haram, masjidku ini (Masjid Nabawi), dan Masjidil Aqsha."*
> — HR Bukhari, no. 1189; Muslim, no. 1397

## Analisis dan Hikmah: Model Toleransi Sepanjang Masa

### Penaklukan Tanpa Pertumpahan Darah

Penaklukan Yerusalem oleh Umar adalah salah satu dari sedikit penaklukan kota suci dalam sejarah yang berlangsung **tanpa pertumpahan darah**. Tidak ada pembantaian, tidak ada penjarahan, tidak ada pemaksaan agama. Bandingkan ini dengan penaklukan Yerusalem oleh Pasukan Salib pada tahun 1099 M — yang diwarnai pembantaian massal terhadap penduduk muslim, Yahudi, dan bahkan Kristen Timur.^1 ^2

### Perjanjian Aelia sebagai Piagam Hak Asasi

Perjanjian Aelia mendahului konsep modern tentang kebebasan beragama dan hak-hak minoritas selama lebih dari seribu tahun. Dokumen ini menjamin:
- Keamanan jiwa dan harta penduduk non-muslim
- Keutuhan gereja-gereja dan tempat ibadah
- Kebebasan beragama tanpa pemaksaan
- Perlindungan hukum yang setara

Para sejarawan Barat, termasuk Karen Armstrong dalam *Jerusalem: One City, Three Faiths*, mengakui bahwa penaklukan Islam atas Yerusalem membawa era toleransi beragama yang jauh lebih baik dibandingkan penguasa-penguasa sebelumnya.

### Kesederhanaan sebagai Kekuatan

Kedatangan Umar yang berjalan kaki, dengan jubah bertambalan dan tanpa rombongan kerajaan, menciptakan kontras dramatis dengan kemegahan Bizantium. Namun justru kesederhanaan inilah yang membuat penduduk Yerusalem — yang telah lama hidup di bawah kemewahan dan kezaliman penguasa Romawi — terkagum dan percaya bahwa penguasa baru mereka adalah orang yang adil.^3

### Perlindungan Hak Umat Kristen

Keputusan Umar menolak salat di dalam Gereja Kebangkitan bukan sekadar tindakan sopan — ia adalah preseden hukum. Dengan menolak salat di sana, Umar mencegah kemungkinan klaim muslim atas gereja tersebut di masa depan. Ini menunjukkan visi jangka panjang seorang pemimpin yang tidak hanya memikirkan masa kini, tetapi juga konsekuensi keputusannya bagi generasi mendatang.^1 ^3

### Mengembalikan Hak Kaum Yahudi

Al-Tabari mencatat bahwa setelah penaklukan, Umar mengizinkan kaum Yahudi untuk kembali tinggal di Yerusalem — hak yang telah dicabut oleh penguasa Romawi dan Bizantium selama berabad-abad.^1 Ini menjadikan penaklukan Islam sebagai momen pembebasan tidak hanya bagi muslim, tetapi juga bagi kaum Yahudi yang telah lama terusir dari kota suci mereka.

## Daftar Pustaka

1. **Al-Tabari, Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Jarir.** *Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk* (Sejarah Para Rasul dan Raja-Raja). Jilid 3-4. Beirut: Dar al-Turats, 1387 H.

2. **Al-Baladhuri, Ahmad ibn Yahya.** *Futuh al-Buldan* (Penaklukan Negeri-Negeri). Beirut: Dar al-Kutub al-Ilmiyyah, 1988.

3. **Ibn Kathir, Ismail ibn Umar.** *Al-Bidayah wa al-Nihayah* (Permulaan dan Akhir). Jilid 7. Beirut: Dar al-Fikr, 1986.

4. Al-Qur'an al-Karim. QS. Al-Isra (17): 1.

penaklukan-mesir.children-en:
# The Conquest of Egypt 🏛️

---

## The Land of the Nile

**Egypt** was a legendary land — the land of pyramids, of the mighty Nile River, and of grand cities. One of its cities, **Alexandria**, was the biggest and most learned city in the world at that time!

But Egypt's native people — called the **Copts** — were suffering terribly. They were oppressed by the Byzantine (Eastern Roman) rulers who forced them to follow a particular version of Christianity. Their church leader, **Patriarch Benjamin**, even had to go into hiding for years to avoid being captured!

In Arabia, a companion of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ named **Amr ibn al-As** had once traveled to Egypt as a merchant. He knew that the people of Egypt were suffering and needed liberation.

🎨 *Illustration: A beautiful view of the Nile River with green fields along its banks. In the distance, the silhouette of a great city with towers and domes. In a corner, a man (Amr) stands atop a sand dune, gazing toward Egypt with determination in his eyes. Dominant colors: Nile blue, field green, sandy gold. Mood: hopeful and determined. Composition: river in center, city in distance, Amr in right corner.*

---

## A Small but Brave Army

Amr asked Caliph **Umar ibn al-Khattab** for permission to conquer Egypt. Umar was hesitant at first — Egypt was enormous and guarded by a powerful Byzantine army. But eventually Umar agreed.

Here's the surprising part: Amr set out with only **4,000 soldiers**! A tiny number to conquer a country as big as Egypt. But Amr was a brilliant leader — he knew that the Egyptian people would welcome him as a liberator, not fear him as a conqueror.

The first battle took place at **Pelusium**, a border fortress. After that, Amr pushed on to **Heliopolis** and won a great victory. Caliph Umar then sent 4,000 more soldiers under **al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam** — an incredibly brave warrior.

🎨 *Illustration: A small cavalry force riding through the gates of a great desert fortress. Banners fluttering in the wind. Though few in number, their faces are full of confidence. On the fortress walls, Byzantine guards watch anxiously. Dominant colors: sandy yellow, bright blue sky, red banners. Mood: brave and confident. Composition: soldiers from left heading toward fortress on right.*

---

## The Fortress of Babylon and the Fall of Alexandria

The hardest battle was the **siege of the Fortress of Babylon** — a massive, powerful Roman fort. The siege lasted **seven months**!

Al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam showed incredible bravery — according to the accounts, he **climbed the fortress walls with a ladder** and opened the gates from inside! With the Fortress of Babylon taken, the road to Alexandria was open.

**Alexandria** — Egypt's largest city and one of the greatest in the world — finally surrendered through a peace treaty. There was no massacre, no destruction. The people were given protection and freedom of worship.

And the happiest moment: **Patriarch Benjamin** could finally come out of hiding! After years of fleeing from Byzantine rulers, he could now lead his people safely. Amr personally guaranteed his protection.

🎨 *Illustration: A great harbor city (Alexandria) with a tall lighthouse by the sea. Ships in the port. At the city gate, Amr shakes hands with an elderly religious leader (Patriarch Benjamin) with a white beard. Residents watch with relief. Dominant colors: sea blue, white city, golden sun. Mood: relief and peace. Composition: city gate in center, sea in background, two figures shaking hands in foreground.*

---

## Fustat — The Tent City

After conquering Egypt, Amr had to choose: where would the new capital be? Many suggested Alexandria, since it was the biggest city. But Amr chose a different spot — near the Fortress of Babylon.

There's a lovely story about why he chose that place: when he was about to march to Alexandria, Amr noticed a **pair of doves nesting on top of his tent**. He didn't want to disturb their nest, so he left the tent standing. That tent became the starting point of a new city called **Fustat** — which means "tent"!

Fustat kept growing and growing — and eventually became the seed of **Cairo**, the capital of Egypt that we know today!

From Egypt also came **Al-Azhar University** — the oldest university in the world that is still running today! Subhanallah!

🎨 *Illustration: A simple tent in the desert near a river. On top of the tent, a pair of doves sit in their nest. Around it, small buildings are beginning to be constructed. In the background, the Nile flows peacefully. Dominant colors: cream tent, blue river, green riverbank. Mood: a humble beginning full of hope. Composition: tent in center, doves on top, construction around it, river in background.*
penaklukan-mesir.children-id:
# Penaklukan Mesir 🏛️

---

## Negeri Sungai Nil

**Mesir** adalah negeri yang sangat terkenal — negeri piramida, negeri Sungai Nil yang panjang, dan negeri dengan kota-kota besar yang megah. Salah satu kotanya, **Alexandria**, adalah kota terbesar dan terpintar di dunia pada masa itu!

Tapi penduduk asli Mesir — yang disebut kaum **Koptik** — sedang sangat menderita. Mereka ditindas oleh penguasa Bizantium (Romawi Timur) yang memaksa mereka mengikuti aliran agama tertentu. Pemimpin gereja mereka, **Patriark Benjamin**, bahkan harus bersembunyi selama bertahun-tahun agar tidak ditangkap!

Di Arabia, seorang sahabat Nabi Muhammad ﷺ bernama **Amr bin al-Ash** pernah berdagang ke Mesir. Ia tahu bahwa rakyat Mesir menderita dan membutuhkan pembebasan.

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Pemandangan indah Sungai Nil dengan ladang hijau di tepinya. Di kejauhan, siluet kota besar dengan menara dan kubah. Di sudut gambar, seorang pria (Amr) berdiri di atas bukit pasir, memandang ke arah Mesir dengan tekad di matanya. Warna dominan: biru Nil, hijau ladang, emas pasir. Mood: penuh harapan dan tekad. Komposisi: sungai di tengah, kota di kejauhan, Amr di pojok kanan.*

---

## Pasukan Kecil yang Berani

Amr meminta izin kepada Khalifah **Umar ibn al-Khattab** untuk menaklukkan Mesir. Umar awalnya ragu — Mesir sangat besar dan dijaga tentara Bizantium yang kuat. Tapi akhirnya Umar mengizinkan.

Yang mengejutkan: Amr berangkat hanya dengan **4.000 pasukan**! Jumlah yang sangat kecil untuk menaklukkan negeri sebesar Mesir. Tapi Amr adalah pemimpin yang sangat cerdas — ia tahu bahwa rakyat Mesir justru akan menyambutnya sebagai pembebas, bukan penakluk.

Pertempuran pertama terjadi di **Pelusium**, benteng perbatasan Mesir. Setelah itu, Amr maju ke **Heliopolis** dan meraih kemenangan besar. Khalifah Umar kemudian mengirim 4.000 pasukan tambahan di bawah pimpinan **al-Zubair bin al-Awwam** — pejuang yang sangat berani.

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Pasukan kecil berkuda memasuki gerbang benteng besar di padang pasir. Bendera berkibar di angin. Meskipun jumlahnya sedikit, wajah mereka penuh percaya diri. Di atas tembok benteng, penjaga Bizantium menyaksikan dengan cemas. Warna dominan: kuning pasir, biru langit cerah, merah bendera. Mood: berani dan percaya diri. Komposisi: pasukan dari kiri menuju benteng di kanan.*

---

## Benteng Babilon dan Jatuhnya Alexandria

Pertempuran paling sulit adalah **pengepungan Benteng Babilon** — benteng Romawi yang sangat besar dan kuat. Pengepungan berlangsung selama **tujuh bulan**!

Al-Zubair bin al-Awwam menunjukkan keberaniannya yang luar biasa — menurut cerita, ia **memanjat tembok benteng dengan tangga** dan membuka gerbang dari dalam! Dengan jatuhnya Benteng Babilon, jalan menuju Alexandria terbuka.

**Alexandria** — kota terbesar di Mesir dan salah satu yang terbesar di dunia — akhirnya menyerah melalui perjanjian damai. Tidak ada pembantaian, tidak ada penghancuran. Penduduknya diberikan perlindungan dan kebebasan beragama.

Dan yang paling membahagiakan: **Patriark Benjamin** akhirnya bisa keluar dari persembunyiannya! Setelah bertahun-tahun bersembunyi dari penguasa Bizantium, ia kini bisa memimpin jemaatnya dengan aman. Amr menjamin keselamatannya secara pribadi.

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Kota pelabuhan besar (Alexandria) dengan mercusuar tinggi di tepi laut. Kapal-kapal di pelabuhan. Di gerbang kota, Amr berjabat tangan dengan seorang pemimpin agama (Patriark Benjamin) berjanggut putih. Penduduk menonton dengan lega. Warna dominan: biru laut, putih kota, emas matahari. Mood: lega dan damai. Komposisi: gerbang kota di tengah, laut di latar belakang, kedua tokoh berjabat tangan di depan.*

---

## Fustat — Kota Tenda

Setelah menaklukkan Mesir, Amr harus memilih: mana yang akan jadi ibu kota baru? Banyak orang menyarankan Alexandria, karena itu kota terbesar. Tapi Amr memilih tempat lain — di dekat Benteng Babilon.

Ada kisah indah tentang kenapa ia memilih tempat itu: ketika hendak berangkat ke Alexandria, Amr melihat **sepasang burung merpati bersarang di atas tendanya**. Ia tidak mau mengganggu sarang burung itu, jadi ia membiarkan tendanya berdiri. Tenda itu menjadi titik awal kota baru yang dinamakan **Fustat** — yang artinya "tenda"!

Fustat kemudian tumbuh dan tumbuh — dan akhirnya menjadi cikal bakal kota **Kairo**, ibu kota Mesir yang kita kenal sekarang!

Dari Mesir juga lahir **Universitas Al-Azhar** — universitas tertua di dunia yang masih berdiri sampai sekarang. Subhanallah!

🎨 *Ilustrasi: Sebuah tenda sederhana di padang pasir dekat sungai. Di atas tenda, sepasang merpati duduk di sarangnya. Di sekitarnya, bangunan-bangunan kecil mulai dibangun. Di latar belakang, Sungai Nil mengalir tenang. Warna dominan: krem tenda, biru sungai, hijau tepi sungai. Mood: awal yang sederhana tapi penuh harapan. Komposisi: tenda di tengah, merpati di atas, pembangunan di sekeliling, sungai di belakang.*
penaklukan-mesir.general-en:
# The Conquest of Egypt (639-641 CE)

## Context: Egypt Under the Byzantine Shadow

Seventh-century Egypt was one of the Byzantine Empire's most vital provinces. The fertile Nile Valley made it the *breadbasket* of Constantinople — the capital's food supply depended heavily on Egyptian agriculture. Alexandria, its great port city, was the largest trading hub in the Mediterranean after Constantinople itself, as well as an intellectual center renowned for its library and scholarly traditions.^1 ^3

Yet beneath the economic prosperity, Egypt's native population — overwhelmingly Coptic Christian — lived under oppression. Al-Baladhuri records in *Futuh al-Buldan* that the Byzantine government imposed harsh religious policies on the Copts, who followed the Monophysite doctrine — at odds with Constantinople's official Orthodoxy.^1 Patriarch Benjamin I, head of the Coptic Church, was forced into hiding for years to escape persecution. Heavy taxation compounded the people's suffering.

Ibn Abd al-Hakam in *Futuh Misr wa Akhbaruha* — the most detailed primary source on the conquest of Egypt — describes widespread discontent among the population.^2 Many Copts viewed the Byzantine rulers not as protectors but as foreign oppressors.

It was here that **Amr ibn al-As** entered the stage of history. Amr was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ renowned for his political acumen and strategic sharpness. He had known Egypt from trading journeys before his conversion to Islam and recognized both the province's potential and its vulnerability.^1 ^2

## Events: A Conquest That Transformed North Africa

### Amr ibn al-As Takes the Initiative (639 CE)

Al-Tabari and al-Baladhuri record that Amr ibn al-As proposed the conquest of Egypt to Caliph **Umar ibn al-Khattab** after the great victories in Syria.^1 ^3 Umar was initially hesitant — Egypt was a large province garrisoned by Byzantine forces, and Muslim resources were already stretched across multiple fronts.

Ibn Abd al-Hakam records a fascinating account of how Amr finally departed: Umar gave him a letter instructing that if he had already entered Egypt when the letter reached him, he could proceed; but if he had not yet crossed the border, he must turn back. Amr, suspecting the letter's contents, **deliberately opened it only after crossing into Egypt** at al-Arish.^2 He read the letter before his troops, then asked: "Brothers, are we in Egypt?" They replied: "Yes." Amr said: "Then we continue as the Commander of the Faithful has ordered."

While the historicity of this account is debated, it captures Amr's initiative and daring — and his cleverness in exploiting a diplomatic opening.

### The Battles of Pelusium and Heliopolis (640 CE)

Amr entered Egypt with a remarkably small force — roughly **4,000 men**.^1 ^2 This was starkly disproportionate to the much larger Byzantine garrison. But Amr relied on speed, surprise, and the support of the local population.

The first battle took place at **Pelusium** (*al-Farama*), the border fortress in northeast Egypt. Al-Baladhuri records that the city fell after a siege of about one month.^1 Amr then advanced south to **Heliopolis** (*'Ayn Shams*), where a major engagement occurred in July 640 CE. Ibn Abd al-Hakam records that the Muslim victory at Heliopolis opened the road to the heart of Egypt.^2

Umar, now convinced of the campaign's viability, dispatched reinforcements of **4,000 additional troops** under the command of al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam — a renowned companion and legendary warrior.^1 ^3

### The Siege of the Fortress of Babylon (640-641 CE)

The most decisive moment in the conquest was the **siege of the Fortress of Babylon** (*Qasr al-Sham'*) — a great Roman fortress located at what is now part of Old Cairo. Al-Tabari records that the siege lasted approximately seven months and was the most difficult military operation of the entire Egyptian campaign.^3

Al-Baladhuri relates that **al-Muqawqis** (Cyrus of Alexandria), the Byzantine governor and Melkite patriarch, attempted to negotiate with Amr.^1 The negotiations were protracted — al-Muqawqis was willing to surrender, but Emperor Heraclius in Constantinople refused and ordered resistance.

The Fortress of Babylon finally fell in April 641 CE after a final assault led by al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, who, according to Ibn Abd al-Hakam, scaled the fortress walls with a ladder and opened the gates from within.^2 ^3

### The Fall of Alexandria (641 CE)

After the Fortress of Babylon fell, Amr marched north toward **Alexandria** — the jewel of the Mediterranean, the city founded by Alexander the Great in 331 BCE, and for centuries the intellectual capital of the ancient world.

Al-Baladhuri records that Alexandria's population — unlike the Coptic inhabitants of the interior — was more cosmopolitan and more attached to Byzantium.^1 The siege lasted several months. However, after the death of Emperor Heraclius in February 641 CE and the political chaos that followed in Constantinople, hopes of reinforcement from the capital evaporated.

Alexandria surrendered by treaty in September 641 CE. Ibn Abd al-Hakam records that the agreement allowed the Byzantine garrison eleven months to evacuate the city peacefully.^2

### The Founding of Fustat

After the conquest, Amr established a new city called **Fustat** near the Fortress of Babylon — rather than making Alexandria his capital. Al-Baladhuri records that this decision was strategic: Fustat was closer to the Arabian Peninsula and was not cut off by the sea, making communication with Madinah easier.^1

Ibn Abd al-Hakam relates a charming story about why Amr chose that particular site: when he was about to depart for Alexandria, he noticed a pair of doves nesting atop his tent. Not wanting to disturb the nest, he left the tent standing — and that tent became the starting point of Fustat.^2 The name *Fustat* itself derives from the Arabic word for "tent."

Fustat eventually grew into a great city and became the precursor of modern Cairo.

## Key Sayings and Scriptural Evidence

Al-Baladhuri records Amr's message to the people of Egypt after the conquest:^1

*"We have come to liberate you. Whoever accepts Islam becomes one of us — with equal rights and equal duties. Whoever does not, pays the jizyah and we protect him."*

Ibn Abd al-Hakam also records Amr's exchange with al-Muqawqis during negotiations. When al-Muqawqis offered gold for the Muslim army to leave, Amr replied:^2

*"We have not come for gold. We come with three choices: Islam, jizyah, or the sword. Choose."*

Umar himself, in his letter to Amr, emphasized the principle of justice:

*"Do not cut down fruit-bearing trees, do not slaughter livestock except for food, and do not wrong the local people. They are your responsibility."*

## Analysis and Lessons: Civilizational Transformation on the Nile

### The Strategic Genius of Amr ibn al-As

The conquest of Egypt showcased the strategic genius of Amr ibn al-As. With an initially tiny force — just 4,000 men — he conquered one of Byzantium's largest provinces. His success rested on several factors: speed of movement, the ability to exploit local discontent, shrewd diplomacy, and impeccable timing — striking while Byzantium was weakened after Yarmuk and the death of Heraclius.^1 ^2

### The Liberation of the Copts

For Egypt's Coptic population, the Islamic conquest was not a catastrophe — it was a **liberation**. Ibn Abd al-Hakam records that Patriarch Benjamin I, who had been in hiding from Byzantine persecution for years, was finally able to emerge and lead his flock openly after Amr guaranteed his safety.^2 Al-Baladhuri adds that the tax imposed by the Islamic administration (*jizyah*) proved lighter than the Byzantine levies that preceded it.^1

### The Birth of Islamic Civilization in Egypt

The founding of Fustat marked the beginning of a new era for Egypt. Within a few generations, Egypt transformed into one of the most important centers of Islamic civilization. From Fustat arose Cairo, which would become the largest city in the Islamic world. From Egypt also emerged Al-Azhar — the world's oldest continuously operating university — along with generations of scholars, scientists, and literary figures who enriched human civilization.

### A Lesson in Leadership

The dynamic between Amr and Umar during the Egyptian campaign illustrates a fascinating tension between field initiative and central authority. Amr demonstrated the courage to take initiative, while Umar showed the magnanimity to support that initiative once convinced of its merit — despite his initial reservations. This is a model of leadership that recognizes the importance of delegation without relinquishing oversight.^1 ^3

## Bibliography

1. **Al-Baladhuri, Ahmad ibn Yahya.** *Futuh al-Buldan* (The Conquest of Lands). Beirut: Dar al-Kutub al-Ilmiyyah, 1988.

2. **Ibn Abd al-Hakam, Abu al-Qasim Abd al-Rahman.** *Futuh Misr wa Akhbaruha* (The Conquest of Egypt and Its History). Cairo: Maktabah Madbouly, 1991.

3. **Al-Tabari, Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Jarir.** *Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk* (The History of Prophets and Kings). Vols. 3-4. Beirut: Dar al-Turath, 1387 AH.
